Clinical and Observational Research: Roots of a Controversy

Edited by Joseph Sandler, Anne-Marie Sandler, Rosemary Davies. Psychoanalytic Monograph 5, Monograph Series of the Psychoanalysis Unit of University College London and the Anna Freud Centre. London: Karnac Books, 2000, 163 pp., $32.50.

Philip Rubovits-Seitz

Philip Rubovits-Seitz

2623 Woodley Place, NW
Washington, DC 20008
E-mail: Rrs2623@gwis2.circ.gwu.edu

Philip Rubovits-Seitz   Training and Supervising Analyst, Columbia University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research; Clinical Professor, Department of Psychiatry, College of Physicians and Surgeons, Columbia University.

This book documents a meeting in London between André Green and Daniel Stern, who debated the controversy between clinical and observational research in psychoanalysis—especially the relevance to psychoanalysis of observing babies and their mothers. The debate is

663

of general interest and is a rich, accessible, and useful presentation of the controversy.

Following an excellent introduction by Riccardo Steiner, an earlier debate, between Green and Robert Wallerstein, presents the controversy in a broader context—the relevance of any formal research to psychoanalysis. Green defines psychoanalysis as focused specifically on the unconscious realm of desire. He argues that most investigations neglect the intrapsychic and the unconscious, and that Freud's topographic model has been misinterpreted in relation to the ego—that the id and the theory of drives have been rejected and replaced by a psychological interpretation of behavior, which leads to oversimplifications. He stresses that transference and the unconscious cannot be observed directly and must therefore be “deduced.” Wallerstein, by contrast, believes we should not give up systematic investigation of analysis, but need to devise methods that do not violate the nature or spirit of psychoanalysis. He disagrees also with Green's restrictive definition of psychoanalysis, preferring a more encompassing view, from the surface to the depths of the mind.

In the main debate, between Green and Stern, the former argues that infant observation cannot access the life of the infant in a way relevant to psychoanalytic thought. Events from that early period can only be reconstructed later, after their occurrence. For Green, the clinical setting is the only place one can attain real analytic knowledge. In addition, he argues, an observer's subjectivity inevitably distorts what is described. (Surprisingly, Stern does not challenge this critique on the ground that psychoanalytic interpretation suffers from the same limitation; for further discussion, see Rubovits-Seitz 1998, 2001). Green continues that no empirically based research can be relevant to psychoanalysis because such research cannot reveal the subject's intrapsychic life. He believes further that empirically based research threatens to destroy psychoanalysis by introducing information from methods that lead to gross oversimplifications. (Missing another opportunity, Stern does not challenge Green's oversimplified definitions of psychoanalysis and psychoanalytic research.)

Paul Dewald   Training Analyst (emeritus) and Supervising Analyst, St. Louis Psychoanalytic Institute; Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, St. Louis University School of Medicine.

In this volume Gail Reed presents a series of essays published at various times during her career, and divided into two parts: “The Clinical Uses of Understanding” and “The Clinical Uses of Comparative Understanding.” The unifying theme throughout is her position that contemporary

712

classical psychoanalytic theory “is the most potentially inclusive, integrative, comprehensive and the least doctrinaire of available theories, though also the most difficult to understand and utilize” (p. xv).

Modern classical theory posits a constructed hierarchy of personal childhood meanings and fantasies based on actual or fantasied experience and conflicts, leading to interpretation of present situations and relationships in terms of compromised unconscious expressions of past ones. Analysis allows the patient to discover this hierarchy and to modify the meanings attributed to present conflicts aid relationships in keeping with more mature, reality-based choices and perceptions instead of the automatic inferences and choices determined by the unconscious hierarchy. Surface content of the patient's associations represents and conceals significant unconscious meaning. The author demonstrates how this theoretical model applies to such topics as reconstruction, the transference and transference neurosis, and issues of meaning and narration in the psychoanalytic situation. These ideas are illustrated with detailed clinical vignettes.

“Any given element,” writes Reed, “may stand for another by virtue of temporal and spatial contiguity as well as of resemblance. An affect may defend against another. Classical theory allows us to understand how and why this may occur. It does not tell us what specifically to find. Classical theory gives us the principles by which meaning becomes disguised and distorted. It tells us that there will be an interaction of wishes, anxiety, and defense. It may even suggest a likely area of wish (e.g., oral or phallic), the kind of dangerous situation generally imagined, the kind of defensive patterns possibly employed. It does not, however, tell us which wishes toward which people or things, which dangerous situations, which defenses, or which current situations stimulate them. It gives us rules for discerning patients' reasons. It does not give us the reasons themselves” (p. 144).

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic Practice and Research

New Explorations and Implications

Stuart Hauser

Stuart Hauser

3 Blackfan Circle
Boston, MA 02115-5794
Fax: 617-730-5440
E-mail: Shauser@jbcc.harvard.edu

Stuart Hauser  

In this issue JAPA launches a new feature, a section focusing on the bidirectional connections between psychoanalytic clinical practice and varied research approaches. Research is not a newcomer to these pages. Over the years, many issues, including the most recent one (J. Amer. Psychoanal. Assn.50/2), have been devoted specifically to research addressing psychoanalytic processes and outcomes, diagnostic variations and meanings, and collaborations with “neighboring sciences” (Holzman and Aronson 1992; Hauser 2002; Tyson 2000). What is different about this new section, which will appear regularly even in issues not dedicated to research studies, is its emphasis on the two-way bridges between clinical theory and observation on the one hand and diverse rigorous research methods—drawn from psychoanalysis as well as from among the social sciences, humanities and neurosciences—on the other.

The two papers in this section are excellent exemplars of what we intend to feature in subsequent issues. Judy Kantrowitz offers evocative narrative data and conceptualizations touching on many aspects of the interactions that occur over the course of a candidate's analysis of a patient. Her exploration of the complex external and internal processes involved in the interplay between candidate, training analyst, supervisor, and patient offers intriguing leads for new systematic research about therapeutic processes, candidate learning, and change in both the treating analyst and the patient.

Exploratory clinical research is clearly not limited to observations and curiosity about psychoanalytic processes and outcomes. Richard Gottlieb provides data from yet another important area: the intersection of biological intervention with psychoanalytic considerations.

937

[[RunningHead]]

JAPA

The First Half-Century

Arnold Richards

Arnold Richards

Arnold Richards  

This issue marks the fiftieth anniversary of JAPA's initial publication. The first issue, volume 1, number 1, appeared in 1953, edited by John Frosch. The journal owes much to Dr. Abram Kagan, then president of International Universities Press, the publisher of JAPA from its inception until 1997. (Dr. Kagan died in 1985, having been succeeded as president of IUP by Martin Azarian. We note with sadness that Mr. Azarian died last fall.)

But the journal had a prehistory. It was preceded by and incorporated the Bulletin of the American Psychoanalytic Association, which under Frosch's editorship began appearing regularly in 1948. The 1948 Bulletin was in fact designated volume 4, as three volumes had appeared before publication was suspended during World War II. In 1987 Frosch published in J. Amer. Psychoanal. Assn.35/2 a wonderful account of the journal's first two decades. He detailed there the political, administrative, and editorial issues that had to be resolved to allow the publication of a first-rate scholarly journal independent of its sponsoring organization. Frosch served as editor for those twenty years, during which he established a level of excellence that was maintained by his successors, Harold Blum and Theodore Shapiro.

We intend our 50th Anniversary volume to examine core issues and trends in psychoanalytic theory, practice, research, and applications. This issue, whose theme is Continuity and Change, deals primarily with theory, but the theme will reverberate in other areas throughout the year. Readers will have an opportunity to assess the state of the psychoanalytic enterprise, recognize its achievements, and note the challenges it faces.

Another Changing of the Guard

Once again we note the changing of the editorial guard. Last year two JAPA associate editors moved on to become editors of distinguished psychoanalytic journals.

5

Of Two Minds: The Growing Disorder in American Psychiatry

T. M. Luhrmann, New York: Alfred A. Knopf, 2000, 337 pp., $27.95.

Elizabeth Auchincloss

Elizabeth Auchincloss

525 East 68th Street
New York, NY 10021
Fax: 212-721-7289

Elizabeth Auchincloss  

When Tanya Luhrmann joined the department of anthropology at the University of California in San Diego in 1989, she was, she tells us, a “relative newcomer” to the field of psychological anthropology. That field, according to her, traces its lineage back to Margaret Mead, who “used a loosely Freudian understanding of childhood experience in different societies to explain their adult behavior” (p. 3). For years, graduate students in anthropology at UCSD had been encouraged to attend lectures on psychoanalysis given to psychiatric residents, and Luhrmann, as a new faculty member, followed suit. She quickly became intrigued with the culture of psychiatric training itself, deciding to make it the subject of her next anthropological investigation. The results of this inquiry are reported in Of Two Minds: The Growing Disorder in American Psychiatry.

Luhrmann reports that the field work for this project, undertaken between 1989 and 1995, was conducted primarily among several groups of psychiatry residents, most of them from two major universities: “a public university on the West Coast” (studied over a period of at least three years) and “a private university on the East Coast” (studied over a period of ten to twelve weeks). As a participant observer of residency training, Luhrmann interviewed residents, sat in on patient evaluations, attended lectures, read assigned texts, and attended professional conferences (including those sponsored by the American Psychiatric Association, the Society for Biological Psychiatry, and the American Psychoanalytic Association). She also interviewed training directors, psychiatrist educators, administrators, and “elite research scientists” from several hospitals.

377

Errant Selves: A Casebook of Misbehavior

Arnold Goldberg. Hillsdale, NJ: Analytic Press, 2000, 217 pp., $39.95.

Philip Ringstrom

Philip Ringstrom

5004 Haskell Avenue
Encino, CA 91436
Fax: 818-906-3269
E-mail: Ringsite@aol.com

Philip Ringstrom   Senior Training and Supervising Analyst, Institute of Contemporary Psychoanalysis, Los Angeles.

Errant Selves: A Casebook of Misbehavior is a wonderful illustration of Arnold Goldberg's seminal elaboration (1995, 1999) of Kohut's concept of vertical splits. His newest volume compiles eight clinical

678

case presentations of the treatment of perversions, delinquencies, and addictions. Errant Selves is especially compelling in its accounts of what happens to therapists on entering the “bowels” of cases of “misbehaviors”—that is, behavioral disorders in which fragile narcissistic cohesion is maintained by a Jekyll-and-Hyde-like reality in which Mr. Hyde performs the very misdeeds that Dr. Jekyll himself might enjoy, there but for the annoying inconvenience of his intervening conscience. Of course, “the magic potion” that creates Hyde's acts of abomination is itself the substance of Jekyll's disavowal, as it is he, the good doctor, who repeatedly reaches for the vial. Robert Louis Stevenson's classic, reintroduced in Goldberg's introduction, captures the essence of how conscious disavowal preserves the vertical split, a process quite different from what is seen in misbehaviors arising from derivatives of unconscious repression.

To say that the act of disavowal is conscious, however, is not to suggest that it is “chosen.” Indeed, Goldberg and his colleagues argue that the misbehaviors documented in the text are matters of necessity: “With our commitment to understanding the symptom rather than treating the behavior, we saw it primarily as necessary for achieving a sense of self-consolidation in our patients” (p. vii) Thus, the patients' behavior disorders were understood as their best adaptation under the circumstances of a paucity of developmental selfobject functions necessary to the regulation of self-esteem and cohesion; the activities manifesting the behavior disorders are then understood as stabilizers of the fragile personality. This interpretation, which is both understanding and compassionate, is juxtaposed with another tone that pervades the book and that is an intermittently moralistic one.

Father Hunger: Explorations with Adults and Children

James Herzog. Hillsdale, NJ: The Analytic Press, 2001, 350 pp., $49.95.

Lynne Rubin

Lynne Rubin

300 Central Park West
New York, NY 10024
Fax: 212-724-3621
E-mail: Rubinls@aol.com

Lynne Rubin   Training and Supervising Analyst, IPA; faculty, Child and Adolescent Training Program, IPTAR.

In childhood imagination, the power of Erlkönig—the King of the Elves—can be lethal. His fearsome influence was the subject of James Herzog's 1980 paper, “Sleep Disturbance and Father Hunger in 18-28-Month-Old Boys: The Erlkönig Syndrome.” And now it is the subject, vastly expanded to be sure, of Father Hunger: Explorations with Adults and Children.

Herzog's work has addressed the psychic implications of the Erlkönig fantasy for a long time. In Goethe's poem “Erlkönig,” as a father ignores the incomprehensible pleas and fears of his young son, the boy dies—in the father's arms—of fright. Herzog's book describes how young boys, without benefit of a father's care, can become victims of their undeciphered fears, and how these fears are manifested in the deprivation syndrome Herzog has labeled “father hunger.”

Father Hunger documents how Herzog uses psychoanalytic intervention to confine the Erlkönig to the realm of childhood imagination. Unaddressed, father hunger can consume a childhood, and can recur in destructive adult sequelae. Here is the focus of this new and important book.

The role of the father in conflicted human psychology has been powerfully addressed by Freud (the demonological neurosis of Christoph Haizmann and the tormented father-son relationship of Schreber being two of his more dramatic case histories), and by Peter Blos, Michael Lamb, Henry Biller, Ernst Abelin, John Munder Ross, and others.

698

The Dream Frontier

Mark J. Blechner. Hillsdale, NJ: The Analytic Press, 2001, 336 pp., $49.95.

Melvin Lansky

Melvin Lansky

10921 Wilshire Boulevard
Suite 509
Los Angeles, CA 90024
Fax: 818-990-3380
E-mail: Mlansky@ucla.edu

Melvin Lansky   Training and Supervising Analyst, Los Angeles Psychoanalytic Institute; Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, UCLA Medical School.

A new psychoanalytic book on the dream, particularly one that is integrative in nature, should be considered in the context of the philosophical and theoretical currents prevalent at the time of its writing. Mark Blechner's The Dream Frontier stands on an important new level of philosophical and theoretical maturity in its synthetic view of thought in dreams and dream formation, clinical work with dreams, and the strength of the psychoanalytic method of working with dreams in relation to other disciplines that study the mind.

This theoretical maturity exists in the wake of a crisis in psychoanalysis that is by no means completely resolved. At the end of the twentieth century we saw a long decrease of interest in the dream as the central or even the paradigmatic phenomenon of psychoanalytic theorizing and practice, together with a dramatic upsurge of interest in the links between psychoanalysis and the cognitive and neurosciences. The result might be seen on the one hand as a breaking loose of psychoanalysis from the canons of authority, and an acquisition of sufficient confidence to welcome collaboration with the laboratory sciences. On the other hand, these accelerating trends within psychoanalysis also seem to be reflective of a perplexity in our psychoanalytic philosophy of science—an unresolved tentativeness about what constitutes our method, and what degree of scientific respectability our theory and praxis merit when they are compared to the mainstream “replicable” empirical sciences of mind and brain. We are in an era when the centrality and value of understanding the workings of the unconscious mind, and the vigorous use of the associative method in pursuit of that understanding, are being challenged—some might even say, rejected. As we contemplate our status amongst the disciplines studying the mind and its workings, we are called upon more and more to justify the status and power of our tools of inquiry in the context of putative data from the replicable sciences.

[[RunningHead]]

Tales of the Unconscious

Alexander Grinstein. Madison, CT: International Universities Press, 343 pp., $57.50.

Morris Peltz

Morris Peltz

3628 Sacramento Street, Suite A
San Francisco, CA 94118
Fax: 415-771-5584

Morris Peltz   Training and Supervising Analyst, San Francisco Psychoanalytic Institute; Assistant Clinical Professor, UCSF.

It is a signal honor for a psychoanalytic writer to have a collection of previously published papers collected into a book. The decision to

1022

compile Grinstein's papers was, to my mind, a very wise one, because essays on applied psychoanalysis are too often written off as merely confirming well-known psychoanalytic theory. Grinstein shows what applied psychoanalysis can be in the hands of a master with vast clinical experience and a gift for meticulous research. The book also includes some clinical studies and a fascinating piece of psychoanalytic detective work.

I will examine Grinstein's four essays on Oscar Wilde in some detail for two reasons: First, his penetrating study of Wilde is probably the best illustration of his method. Second, Grinstein attempts to answer a question that still boggles the mind: Why at the height of his career (he had just completed An Ideal Husband and The Importance of Being Earnest, both produced in 1895) did Wilde engage the Marquess of Queensberry in the disastrous litigation that led ultimately to his imprisonment and ruin? What unconscious guilt or need for punishment overrode all common sense? Wilde lied to his counsel when he brought the criminal libel suit against Queensberry, claiming that there was absolutely no truth to the latter's allegations that Wilde was a sodomite. And why, after the second trial, when the noose was palpably tightening, did Wilde refuse to flee England in spite of the entreaties of his counsel, his wife, and his friends that he do so?

In his first paper Grinstein examines The Picture of Dorian Gray, published in 1891, one year before Wilde began his disastrous relationship with Lord Alfred Douglas, Queensberry's son. It appears that Wilde had undergone some personality changes in the three years preceding the publication of the novel. Colleagues who knew him said he had visibly changed, and become unpleasant and bloated. He was haunted by unrequited desire. “In his Letters from Reading Gaol,” Grinstein writes, “Wilde himself indicated that he had been ‘suffering from the most horrible form of erotomania which made him forget his wife and children, his high social position in London and Paris … and left him the helpless prey of the most revolting passions’” (p. 213).

Yes, we are psychoanalysts, and of course you may join us, but you know, we're just barmy people treating barmy people!

—Frances Tustin

So Judith Mitrani, with a twinkle in her eye, opens her book. “Of course you may join us”? Who wouldn't hesitate with such an introduction? Yes, it is true that we do barmy work, but to an American, the Kleinians are perhaps the barmiest of psychoanalysts—they have been referred to as “connoisseurs of hatred”—and they treat the barmiest of patients—those considered “unanalyzable” (p. 6), or surely less than ideal for our usual psychoanalytic technique.

Impact of Narcissism: The Errant Therapist on a Chaotic Quest

Peter L. Giovacchini. Northvale, NJ: Jason Aronson, 2000, xvi + 324 pp., $40.00

Richard Waugaman

Richard Waugaman

4701 Willard Avenue #204

Richard Waugaman   Training and Supervising Analyst Emeritus, Washington Psychoanalytic Institute; Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Georgetown University School of Medicine.

Giovacchini tackles a number of interrelated topics in this stimulating book. His title aptly condenses several of his interests—the impact of narcissism on the patient; the impact of narcissism on the analyst; and the impact that the narcissism of each has on the other. Giovacchini won me over in his preface, where he eloquently laments the loss of what he calls the “intrapsychic focus” in contemporary psychoanalysis. Although the author has himself challenged traditional analytic beliefs during his long and productive career, he feels that we have gone too far in abandoning some of Freud's core discoveries about the role of unconscious conflicts in psychopathology. For example, he cites reductionistic theories of pathogenesis that restrict themselves to childhood trauma alone or organic factors alone. He argues that proponents of some analytic movements are motivated primarily by narcissistic needs to challenge the status quo with their innovations. Although he characterizes his attitude toward classical theory as “ambivalent,” Giovacchini might agree with Leo Rangell (1985), who advocates a total composite theory rather than the pars pro toto fallacy of so many contemporary analytic theories that select one perspective on the mind and dismiss all others. Giovacchini gets impatient when he feels that the proponents of ostensibly new contributions to the field ignore the similar ideas of their predecessors. At the same time, he fails to mention attachment theory and newer thinking about dissociative identity disorder, both of which are relevant to much of his clinical material.

Giovacchini alludes to one of the forces that breed intolerance in our field: the quasireligious nature of various analytic orthodoxies and heterodoxies. He writes of analysts who venerate Freud as an “icon” (p. 8), of the “religious atmosphere” of strict Freudians (p. 13), and of the elevation of Freud or founders of competing schools to “sainthood” (p. 62). One way of interpreting our “continuous and shameful internecine warfare” (p. 13) would be as religious wars. Bertrand Russell apparently quipped that people are only willing to die for things they feel deeply uncertain about.1

According to the author's preface, this book is a summing up of his forty-five years of clinical teaching experience (p. xi). As such, he offers the reader a view of the way a modern-day classical Freudian analyst thinks and works. Chessick is unhappy with much of the recent psychoanalytic literature, which he feels does not modify and correct Freud's paradigm, but tends to replace it entirely. He sees this as a mistake, and thinks that “we should retain Freud's basic ideas as our central paradigm and starting point” (p. xii).

For Chessick, everything in the mind and even in character formation is a compromise between drives and repressing forces. He is especially critical of interpersonal and intersubjective theory. Despite the fact that he writes in a most scholarly fashion and is very conversant with the literature, he seems to misunderstand current inter-subjectivity theory as put forward by Levine and Friedman (2000).

1082

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Andrea Del Sarto Rehabilitated

A Psychoanalytic Emendation

Harry Trosman

Harry Trosman

5841 South Maryland Avenue
Chicago, IL 60637-1470
Fax: 773-702-6454
E-mail: Htrosman@yoda.bsd.uchicago.edu

Harry Trosman  

Ernest Jones's “The Influence of Andrea del Sarto's Wife on His Art” (1913) is an early example of psychoanalysis applied to the study of a prominent painter. Greatly influenced by Freud's Leonardo da Vinci and a Memory of His Childhood, Jones gave excessive credence to Vasari's highly prejudicial account of the life of del Sarto, on which the study relied heavily. Jones attempted to account psychologically for the circumstance that del Sarto, though highly skilled and “faultless,” was not the equal of the three preeminent masters of the Italian High Renaissance: Leonardo, Michelangelo, and Raphael. Jones's uncritical acceptance of the Vasari biography encouraged him to view del Sarto's assumed deficiency as the result of excessive attachment to his wife, a pathological uxoriousness. A contemporary psychoanalytic perspective, with its emphasis on the emotive response of the analyst, requires us to pay attention to the evocative nature of the work of the artist, an approach Jones neglected. In an examination of several paintings, the artist's sensitivity to the position of the spectator is explored, as is the interest in involving the viewer spatially and emotionally. An appreciation for the viewer's position is consistent with a capacity for using projected internal objects for creative purposes. The presence of this capacity suggests a revised view of del Sarto's contribution to art and of his relationship with his wife.

[[RunningHead]]

Whose Freud? The Place of Psychoanalysis in Contemporary Culture.

Peter Brooks and Alex Woloch. New Haven: Yale University Press, 2000, 349 pp., $19.95.

Jeremy Mack

Jeremy Mack

10 East 96th Street
New York, NY 10128
Fax: 212-327-2436
E-mail: jrm10e@aol.com

Jeremy Mack  

Whose Freud? The Place of Psychoanalysis in Contemporary Culture, edited by Peter Brooks and Alex Woloch, is a collection of essays—first presented in 1998 at a Yale symposium of the same name—dealing with “the status of psychoanalysis as a discipline and discourse in contemporary culture.” The guiding questions for the editors were the present state of psychoanalysis—in what manner or form it survives in our culture—and whether there has been a wholesale displacement of psychoanalysis from the area of science into the universities. The essays are organized into six topics: (1) psychoanalysis and its discontents; (2) psychoanalysis: between therapy and hermeneutics; (3) psychoanalysis and sexual identity; (4) psychoanalysis and historiography; (5) psychoanalysis and theories of mind; and (6) psychoanalysis and truth. The twenty-three contributors, who include Judith Butler, Frederick Crews, Jonathan Lear, Robert Lifton, Robert Michels, and Juliet Mitchell, are a distinguished group, fourteen of whom hold endowed chairs at prestigious universities. All of the essays are dense, and at least some are worthy of the work of explication involved in reading them.

In “The Vortex beneath the Story,” Juliet Mitchell discusses the proposition that what she terms “the trauma and the story” are the cause and solution of psychic ill-being. To this end she posits a trauma—a breach of the protective shield around the neonate—that causes a gap in development, an “effraction” similar to Winnicott's “impingement.” When this occurs, the gap becomes filled by hallucinations—of the nourishing breast, for example—according to the need of the subject. These hallucinations become, for the subject and the respondent, “the story” that fills the gap.

1391

[[RunningHead]]

Darwin's Worms

Adam Phillips. New York: Basic Books, 2000, 148 pp., $20.00

Gerald Fogel

Gerald Fogel

2250 NW Flanders, Suite 301
Portland, OR 97210
Fax: 503-223-5802
E-mail: Geraldfogel@attbi.com

Gerald Fogel  

Many of Adam Phillips's critically acclaimed books are collections of essays originally written for literary journals or given as talks to academic audiences. Often he writes directly about psychoanalysis and psychoanalysts, but all of his writings involve psychoanalytic ideas and perspectives. His language and style reflect the cutting-edge sensibilities of contemporary cultural and intellectual life. His writing is engaging and conversational, but also highly creative and literate. Often he begins with observations on commonplace subjects, then quickly takes his well-educated readers directly to profound human issues and questions.

In Darwin's Worms, Phillips presents two such essays—one on Darwin (“Darwin Turns the Worm”) and another on Freud (“The Death of Freud”). Although we are never explicitly told so, I suspect that these two essays were originally intended as stand-alone works. By adding a prologue and an epilogue, both substantial, the author expands two brief works into a short book, an extended meditation on the attitudes toward life, working habits, great discoveries, and transcendent values that Phillips believes create a kinship between these two great thinkers, whose ideas so profoundly influenced Western thought in the last century.

As a book, Darwin's Worms works best in the prologue and epilogue, where the author synthesizes Darwin's and Freud's ideas and their implications. Phillips points out that Darwin and Freud are each known for making a rigorous, compelling case that human beings are part of nature. Both men undermined humanity's illusions regarding its privileged place in the universe or its special relationship with a divine being. Darwin's religious faith and Freud's avowed atheism may seem at odds. Nevertheless, each played a crucial role in the rise of secular humanism and a contemporary worldview that no longer requires that we take God into consideration when examining our assumptions about human nature and the ultimate meaning of human life.

[[RunningHead]]

Infant Research and Adult Treatment: Co-Constructing Interactions

Beatrice Beebe and Frank M. Lachmann. Hillsdale, NJ: Analytic Press, 2002, 280 pp., $47.50.

Ruth Imber

Ruth Imber

390 West End Avenue, Suite 1-H
New York, NY 10024
E-mail: Rimberphd@aol.com

Ruth Imber   Training and Supervising Analyst, William Alanson White Psychoanalytic Institute.

Owing to the efforts of infant researchers over the last several decades, we have been learning a lot about the ways in which babies develop from birth through early childhood. A vast amount of information is taken in and processed long before the acquisition of language is apparent. Much of what has been learned from the empirical studies of babies has forced psychoanalysts to revise their developmental theories. For example, Daniel Stern's work on the origins of the sense of self (1985) enabled him and his coworkers to reject Mahler's developmental schema, with its notion of an early autistic stage. Analysts who were reared on this developmental model had to revise their ideas about when infants begin to experience their own subjectivity, as well as when they are capable of interacting with their environment, human and otherwise-far earlier, it turns out, than had been imagined. Infant Research and Adult Treatment: Co-constructing Interactions likewise contributes greatly to our understanding of how infants interact with their caretakers. In addition, the authors ambitiously invite us to rethink some of our assumptions about what it is we do with our patients that leads to change. It seems to me they succeed admirably.

Beatrice Beebe, the reader is informed, began her infant research career working with Stern and has used close examination of the face-to-face interaction of mother and infant to measure the subtle bi-directional influences that occur in the dyad. The authors review a large body of research, including their own, to demonstrate the idea that infants are continually engaged in the internalization of stimuli from the inanimate environment and from their caregivers, which they internalize in ways that modify their self-regulating processes. Simultaneously, the infant is also engaged in constant interactive regulatory processes that in turn have an impact on the interaction with the caregiver and on the caregiver's self-regulation. The authors make clear that babies are not simply passive recipients but are very active participants in the “dialogue” with their caregivers long before verbal language is acquired. While many of the studies they cite (including their own) will probably be familiar to readers well versed in the empirical infant literature, others are in for a treat.

Three Seductive Ideas

Jerome K. Kagan Cambridge: Harvard University Press, 1998, 232 pp., $14.95.

Arnold Wilson

Arnold Wilson

257 West 17th Street, #4A
New York, NY 10001
Fax: 973-746-7986
E-mail: ArnoldWilson@compuserve.com

Arnold Wilson  

Jerome Kagan is Daniel and Amy Starch Professor of Psychology at Harvard University. Aside from holding a prestigious endowed chair, he is a developmental psychologist of the first rank, who has made important contributions to our understanding of child development, Piagetian theory, and the theoretical underpinnings of life span developmental processes. As tends to be the case in academic social science these days, his interests range broadly over a variety of interesting topics, with no clear disciplinary boundaries confining his thinking.

Because academic research today often resists translation into the folk psychology of everyday life, Kagan has taken it upon himself to write a book easily read by the general public. This volume attempts to strike a balance between loyalty to intellectual rigor and the canons of academic research, on the one hand, and, on the other, the wish to share ideas with nontechnical readers. Kagan does a fine job of managing this tension, and has produced a book that can be read and easily understood by most psychoanalysts, even those not schooled in developmental psychology or sophisticated in research. It is specifically the research Kagan has conducted over the course of his career that he is attempting to distill and make available to a wider audience.

The three seductive ideas alluded to in the title are that (1) most psychological processes generalize broadly; (2)

[[RunningHead]]

On Freud's Couch: Seven New Interpretations of Freud's Case Histories

Edited by Iréne Matthis and Imre Szecsödy. Northvale, NJ: Jason Aronson, 1998, xii + 267 pp., $50.

Zvi Lothane

Zvi Lothane

1435 Lexington Avenue
New York, NY 10128
Fax: 212-534-5555
E-mail: Schreber@lothane.com

Zvi Lothane   Clinical Associate Professor of Psychiatry, Mount Sinai School of Medicine.

Freud's case histories, and the one by Breuer: what are they to us today? Holy relics and fetishes for faithful Freudians, museum memorabilia for the masses, or, as of late, fodder for Freud-bashers? Jacob Arlow has pleaded for their relegation to the analytic attic, on the grounds that the whole lot of them is both dated and flawed. I disagree. Flawed they may be, but interest in them should never fade. For these legendary cases, coeval with the creation of psychoanalysis more than a century ago, remain an integral part of a discipline that is defined and determined by its history, and so should preserve its history. That conviction is clearly demonstrated in this book. It is a revisiting of seven Freud's cases, a labor of love by six Freudian analysts from Sweden: the above-named editors, Johan Norman, Rolf Künstlicher, Lars Sjögren, and Andrzej Werbert.

Now a case history, like a painting or sculpture, is but a distillate of clinical work. It is a facet or a part abstracted from the whole, real, historical person who loved, lived, and suffered and also free associated and resisted on the couch, involved in a real and transferential relationship with the analyst. In this way the couch gives birth to unique personal histories. And these particular cases, formerly disguised, came back to life long after they were published as subjects of ever widening posthumous historical and biographical investigations. Thus Anna O. returned as Bertha Pappenheim, Elisabeth von R. as Ilona Weiss, Emmy von N. as Fanny von Moser, Katharina as Aurelie Clothilde Katharina Kronich, Cäcilie M. as Anna von Lieben, Little Hans as Herbert Graf, Dora as Ida Bauer, the Rat Man as Ernst Lanzer, and the Wolf Man as Sergey Pankeyev. The only unidentified case in this book is Lucy R.

Kantrowitz on Her Greenberg Commentary

Judy Kantrowitz

Judy Kantrowitz

334 Kent Street
Brookline, MA 02446
Fax: 617-277-9564
E-mail: Judy_kantrowitz@hms.harvard.edu

Judy Kantrowitz  

August 8, 2001. I wish to clarify a statement of mine that Jay Greenberg (J. Amer. Psychoanal. Assn.49/2) has misinterpreted. He contrasts my statement, that we risk retraumatizing the patient if we do not do something new, with Patrick Casement's statement that we traumatize patients when we try to do something new. Actually, my thinking is similar to Casement's. My intended meaning was that we retraumatize patients when we fail to understand what they are communicating and so inadvertently engage in action (or inaction) that they interpret as a repetition of earlier traumatic experiences. Casement describes patients who are in distress or who have difficulty managing strong feelings. If the parent or caretaker has been unable to bear and contain these strong feelings, and if we as analysts do not understand the extent of the distress, and ourselves seem unable to bear and contain these feelings, then we are repeating an earlier traumatic experience. I believe that this is a kind of retraumatization for the patient. I was not suggesting that as analysts we should do anything out of the ordinary or engage in “exceptional maneuvers,” as Casement puts it; I meant only that failing to contain what was previously experienced as uncontainable, or failing to understand and articulate what was not understood, or was left unaddressed, is likely to be more harmful in its effect than any active attempt to do something different. However, my own way of working, when I am being my best analytic self, is not to undertake “exceptional maneuvers” but rather to listen, reflect, clarify, and interpret. Of course, I get caught by countertransference reactions, life stresses, and other such phenomena, and end up enacting and responding to patients' unconscious pressures on me. While I view this as an inevitable part of the work, I do not valorize it. If these reactions are understood and processed with the patient, very productive work may well emerge. That is why I think that these reactions are often less harmful than the quiet repetitions that the analyst does not recognize and the patient may not put into words. But these behaviors are unconscious “enactments,” not conscious maneuvers to do something different.

Soul Murder Revisited: Thoughts about Therapy, Hate, Love, and Memory

Leonard Shengold. New Haven: Yale University Press, 1999, 328 pp., $45 hardcover, $18.95 paperback.

Howard Levine

Howard Levine

124 Dean Road
Brookline, MA 02445
E-mail: Hblevine@aol.com

Howard Levine   faculty, Psychoanalytic Institute of New England, East and Massachusetts Institute for Psychoanalysis.

Leonard Shengold occupies a unique position in contemporary American psychoanalysis. At a time when analytic discourse was underemphasizing the possibility of incest and childhood sexual abuse, he raised an articulate and compelling voice. His early writings about the powerfully evocative concept of soul murder helped alert us to the fact and consequences of severe childhood trauma and neglect (Shengold 1979). Ten years later, he presented many clinical experiences

1068

with, thoughts about, and recommendations for the treatment of severely abused and neglected patients in Soul Murder: The Effect of Childhood Abuse and Deprivation(Shengold 1989). The present volume, which appears after the passage of ten more years and the publication of four subsequent books and innumerable papers, offers readers the benefits of the author's further thoughts on and experiences with this very important subject.

Given the polemics and reductionism to which the subject of child abuse and its consequences is often subjected, it is most gratifying to note that this author can maintain a deep appreciation of the complexity of the issues involved. From the outset, Shengold cautions that “the problem of differentiating fantasy from memory remains formidable for therapist and patient alike” (p. 7). Thus, he proposes that the possibility of adult and parental seduction “should neither be assumed nor denied” (p. 7). This need for caution arises in part from the nature of memory. “We can no longer believe in memory as the simple registration of what has actually happened in the past” (p. 10). Quoting Oliver Sacks (1997), Shengold suggests that memory “is not a recording but an active process of recategorization—of reconstruction, of imagination, determined by our own values and perspectives” (pp. x-xii), and, he adds, “by our psychological conflicts and complexes” (p. 10), as well.

For Shengold, the fact of a patient's having undergone real trauma does matter: “Having had to live through overwhelmingly bad experiences makes for qualitative differences in the developing mental life of any individual” (p. 8).

[[RunningHead]]

Freud's Encounter with Cocaine

Louis Linn

Louis Linn

1148 Fifth Avenue
New York, NY 10128
E-mail: LousLinnMd@aol.com

Louis Linn  

This essay is a tribute honoring the Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association on the occasion of its fiftieth anniversary. When I was invited to contribute to this anniversary volume as one of only three surviving authors in the pages of volume 1 of the journal, I must confess that I was seized initially with serious misgivings. It pleased me to be in the company of Jacob Arlow and Leo Rangell, two truly distinguished colleagues (who are also dear friends). It is no false modesty to say that while their place in these special pages is truly meritorious, my own presence here is connected in significant degree to my longevity.

In any event, I reacquainted myself with my journal article (Linn 1953). That paper's conclusion is that in patients who are charactero-logically predisposed to the use of denial, organic interferences with perception facilitate denial. Conversely, the need to deny can result in functional disturbances in perception (inexact perception). I discussed the relationship of denial to other defense mechanisms as well. My paper concludes with the statement that alcohol and drugs can plunge a person into a pharmacologically induced dreamy state in which perception and recent memory are impaired and in which denial is facilitated. In short, I was suggesting how organic brain changes can affect purely psychological defense mechanisms and in this way explain some of the clinical aspects of addictive states. I wrote that paper while serving as liaison psychiatrist on the neurology service of Mount Sinai Hospital in New York City. It was one of several papers I wrote during those years in which I tried to explore the mind-brain interface, an interest that continues to this day (Linn 1948, 1953, 1954a, b, c, 1955, 1958, 1960, 1974, 1986, 1987, 1989, 2000; Linn and Stein 1944, 1945; Linn, Cohen, and Zimmerman 1945; Linn 1948; Linn and Goldman 1949

Life Member, American Psychoanalytic Association. Submitted for publication August 15, 2002.

[[RunningHead]]

Continuity and Change in Psychoanalytic Theory

Bonnie Litowitz

Bonnie Litowitz

180 North Michigan Avenue
Suite 2220
Chicago, IL 60601
E-mail: Belitowitz@aol.com

Bonnie Litowitz  

Se vogliamo che tutto rimanga come è, bisogna che tutto cambi.

If we want things to stay as they are, things will have to change.

—Tomasi Di Lampedusa, The Leopard

Plus ça change, plus c 'est la même chose.

The more things change, the more they stay the same.

—French Proverb

In its more than one-hundred-year history, psychoanalysis has undergone many changes. Yet some concepts endure, and these may be considered defining, distinguishing psychoanalysis from other forms of treatment and from other theories of how the mind works. While there is general agreement on this fact, there is more controversy over what precisely are the unique contributions of psychoanalysis.

For fifty years this journal has recorded both the history and the debates that have dominated our profession, especially as it has been practiced in the United States. This issue, number one, begins our fiftieth anniversary year by examining change and continuity in psychoanalytic theory.

Freud's training as a neurologist informed his early work on aphasia. He brought that knowledge to bear on the new set of clinical problems he encountered, first in Paris and then in Vienna: the hysterias. His Project for a Scientific Psychology (1895) documents his attempt to understand psychological processes from a neurophysio-logical perspective. He abandoned that project because he could not fashion, out of the knowledge of the brain then available to him, the explanations for mind that he was seeking.

[[RunningHead]]

A Psychoanalysis for Our Time: Exploring the Blindness of the Seeing I

Jeffrey B. Rubin. New York: New York University Press, 1998, xiv + 255 pp., $38.00

Irwin Hirsch

Irwin Hirsch

1311-1327 Lexington Avenue, Suite 1A
New York, NY 10128
Fax: 212-876-8812
E-mail: Kynderino@aol.com

Irwin Hirsch  

In a book that is largely original and has many virtues, Jeffrey Rubin sets his sights on analyzing the profession and the culture of psychoanalysis. He possesses a strong affinity, indeed a passion, for psychoanalytic perspectives of most all stripes, for at their best he views them all as profoundly liberating, for patients and practitioners alike. He argues convincingly, however, that like psychoanalytic patients, psychoanalysis as a profession and those who practice it have not reached their potentials because of a pervasive conflict between the anxiety of seeing and facing novelty, in contrast with holding on to what is known, safe, and comfortable. He points to what he sees as a major contradiction between a liberating theory devoted to seeing the truth at all costs and a psychoanalytic culture that is often intolerant, dogmatic, and blind—thus, “the blindness of the seeing I.” Rubin asserts that psychoanalysis has always been hindered by the idealization of charismatic individuals, a tendency that leaves the profession vulnerable to the inevitable psychopathology of those leaders. He summarizes aspects of the life and work of Freud, Winnicott, and Kohut and speculates how their unresolved personal conflicts are evident in what he considers the limitations of their theories. These theoretical blind spots, he argues, are then perpetuated by those who treat their theoretical fathers as religious figures, what Rubin calls “ancestor worship.” It follows logically that psychoanalytic theories then stagnate as hidden religions, promoting idolatry, deference to authority, and intolerance toward competing theoretical ideas. He cites the tragedy of Ferenczi's fall from grace as one egregious example of what can happen to creative deviants within a strong theoretical culture.

372

The Bi-Personal Field: Experiences in Child Analysis

Antonino Ferro. Edited by Elizabeth Bott Spillius. London: Routledge, 1999, 232 pp., $32.95.

Nydia Lisman-Pieczanski

Nydia Lisman-Pieczanski

4417 36th Street NW
Washington, DC 20008
Fax: 202-363-1909
E-mail: Albertop@erols.com

Nydia Lisman-Pieczanski   child and adult psychoanalyst in private practice, Washington, DC.

Working with children and young people has been an uphill road. Analysts complain that it requires too much energy, that it is “not the real thing,” and, unfortunately, not much has been written on child analysis since the seminal contributions of the Kleinian school and Anna Freud.

Antonino Ferro presents us a real gift: a demonstration of his rare clinical skills, his detailed observation of the emotional encounter between patient and analyst, and a thorough understanding of the interplay between transference and countertransference. Since the time of Melanie Klein and Arminda Aberastury (whom I had the privilege to know well in Argentina), I haven't come across such a detailed description of the emotional experience of work with children and young people, with an eye to both analyst and patient.

Ferro's book has eight chapters and a very useful introduction by Elizabeth Bott Spillius, who, because Ferro assumes the reader's familiarity with the writings of Klein and Bion, explains Klein's concepts of positions and their fluctuations and Bion's elaboration of projective identification. She presents these as part of the mode of communication between patient and analyst, as they are originally between mother and infant. In her introduction, Spillius discusses some of Ferro's differences with the British Kleinian school and her own views regarding these differences.

695

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Evolution of Psychoanalysis: Contemporary Theory and Practice

John Gedo. New York: Other Press, 1999, 300 pp., $25.00 paperback.

Glen Gabbard

Glen Gabbard

One Baylor Plaza
Houston, TX 77030
Fax: 713-798-3138
E-mail: Ggabbard12@aol.com

Glen Gabbard  

In this volume John Gedo undertakes an ambitious project. He sets out to trace the intellectual history of psychoanalysis between 1973 and 1998 by reviewing the sixty most influential books of that era. The author, former book review editor of this journal and prolific contributor to the field, is ideally suited to take on such a challenge. Nevertheless, when I read in the introduction to the volume that the author planned to “present reviews of the sixty featured monographs, arranged topic by topic, forming an annotated bibliography of the core psychoanalytic contributions of the past generation” (p. xiii), I experienced a sinking feeling. Would this be a tedious and plodding journey through a series of sequential book reviews masquerading as a coherent book?

To my great delight, my fears proved unfounded. Gedo does not really “review” the books—rather, he engages their authors' ideas. He succinctly summarizes the key points made by the contributors he selects, usually in an evenhanded way, and places their thinking in the context of the entire field. He also provides critiques of these ideas from his own perspective, the bias of which he readily acknowledges. He gives credit long overdue to writers as diverse as Herbert Rosenfeld and Benjamin Rubenstein, both of whom are neglected by American analysts. He provides a masterful yet succinct critique of current French psychoanalysis. He is respectful and admiring of Paul Gray's work, while stressing that the technique it advances is limited to a narrow spectrum of patients. He warns repeatedly of the perils of divorcing psychoanalytic theories from the data of the neurosciences.

After his erudite survey, he provides a brief concluding chapter in which he points out the thematic threads in current psychoanalytic discourse. He emphasizes the need to avoid Cartesian dualism and to make use of empirical evidence from extraclinical sources, as well as from the consulting room. He ends with a clarion call to link the theory of technique to what is known about mental functioning.

[[RunningHead]]

James Joyce and Molly Bloom

Reflections on Their Relationship

Theodore Jacobs

Theodore Jacobs

170 East 77th Street
Apt. IG
New York, NY 10021
Fax: 914-725-5877
E-Mail: Theojmd@aol.com

Theodore Jacobs  

The relationship between James Joyce and his memorable creation, Molly Bloom, is explored in relation to Joyce's remarkable creativity and various factors that may have contributed to it. A character forged primarily out of Joyce's perceptions of his wife Nora and memories of his mother, Molly also contains aspects of Joyce's warded-off and wished-for self-representation. A focus on both biographical and dynamic contributions to the creation of Molly helps to illuminate aspects of Joyce's psychology

responseSet.title: Psychoanalysis and Evil

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis and Evil

Richard Gottlieb

Richard Gottlieb

263 West End Avenue
Apt I-E
New York, NY 10023
E-mail: Rmgottlieb@aol.com

Richard Gottlieb  

The Reproduction of Evil: A Clinical and Cultural Perspective. By Sue Grand. Hillsdale, NJ: The Analytic Press, 2000, 216 pp., $36.00.

The Evil We Do: The Psychoanalysis of Destructive People. By Carl Goldberg. Prometheus Books, 2000, 260 pp., $25.00.

It is perhaps illustrative of the extent to which our mental health professions have succeeded to the place of the priesthood in our society that in a recent Newsweek cover story, “Evil: What Makes People go Wrong?” (see Begley 2001) eight of the nine authorities interviewed and quoted were mental health professionals. None offered more than banal generalizations, leaving the thoughtful reader disappointed and skeptical.

Of course, it is hardly self-evident that psychoanalysts are especially qualified to understand evil. Some among us acknowledge outright that evil is beyond our ken. For example, Twemlow (2000), in rejecting Al Gore's characterization of the Columbine school massacre as “evidence of evil,” wrote that “the idea of evil places it beyond the pale of human understanding and thus beyond a possibility of resolution without divine intervention” (p. 776). There is a literature that occupies a hazily defined interface between religion and psychiatry that purports to deal more confidently with the problem of evil. For Peck (1983), for example, where evil is his patient's problem, procedures such as exorcism or deliverance may be the prescription (see especially chap. 5).

However, the study of fantasies of evil can readily be considered a proper subject for psychoanalytic investigation. Freud, in his little-read essay (1923) on demonic possession and exorcism, concluded that for the case at hand the Evil One, the Devil, represented a distorted version of the father of his childhood and a reprise of the primal father of human prehistory. Religious material, as well as artistic, folkloric, and musical works, can be fruitfully analyzed as sources of fantasy.

1329

[[RunningHead]]

Memory, Brain and Belief

Daniel L. Schacter and Elaine Scarry. Cambridge: Harvard University Press, 2000, hardcover 320 pp., $42.00; paperback 368 pp., $20.00.

Regina Pally

Regina Pally

11980 San Vincente Boulevard
Suite 810
Los Angeles CA 90049
Fax: 323-857-3328
E-mail: Rpally@ucla.edu

Regina Pally  

Neuroscience makes it increasingly clear that nonconscious brain mechanisms influence conscious perception, memory, and behavior. From the psychoanalytic perspective, however, a significant gap remains in our understanding of how these neural processes result in the motivated defense mechanisms of a dynamic unconscious. This book, based on presentations given at a conference sponsored by Harvard University in 1997, narrows the gap. It deals with some rather complicated neuroscientific concepts but is well worth the effort it takes to read.

The volume's central assumption is that what we recall is organized to be consistent with our beliefs, and the beliefs we form may be shaped by our memory of past events. This is so because memory is an active constructive process: What we recall is not what we actually experienced, but rather a reconstruction of what we experienced that is consistent with our current goals and knowledge about the world. If we remember something often enough, we will likely believe it to be true and real. A brief summary of three chapters will convey a sense of how this book addresses its subject.

In their chapter, Johnson and Raye emphasize that source monitoring can regularly lead to memory distortion. Source monitoring is a determination about the accuracy of recall and whether a memory is the result of personal experience—of one's having seen it, read or heard about it, or simply imagined it.

362

responseSet.title: Apropos Ahumada on Lacan
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Apropos Ahumada on Lacan

Mitchell Wilson

Mitchell Wilson

E-mail: Mdwmd@home.com

Mitchell Wilson  

December 27, 2000. I am writing regarding Jorge Ahumada's book review essay, “The Private and the Public: Psychoanalysis as ‘Letter”’ (J. Amer. Psychoanal. Assn.48/3). I read the essay with anticipation and interest, especially given its author's well-deserved place within the international psychoanalytic community. Unfortunately, I was disappointed and dismayed by both the tone and the content of his essay.

In the conventional book review essay, the writer places a premium on clear explication and summary of the main ideas under consideration. In Ahumada's case, he does not seem interested in teaching or explaining. With a smugness of tone that echoes the great Lacan himself, Ahumada gives the reader—especially the uninitiated reader—little chance to grasp much of anything, because he doesn't start with first principles and assumptions. Instead his essay gets off to an unfortunate beginning because he emphasizes the political and fashionable side of Lacanianism, rather than the development of a theoretical and clinical body of thought. After all, Lacan started writing in the 1930s and teaching his seminar in the early 1950s, long before he became a “celebrity.”

In terms of content, basic Lacanian ideas get buried in the crowded middle of the essay. They are buried enough that the naive reader would have no way of knowing whether an idea was important or not. For example, one finds in the middle of a paragraph on the eighth page of the essay a single sentence about Lacan's concept of the minor stage. Yet Lacan's entire oeuvre starts from this idea of the minor stage and “dual relations.” Lacan asserts that psychoanalysis is not about imaginary or dual engagements (however inevitable such interactions may be); that is, psychoanalysis is not dyadic but fundamentally triadic. For Lacanians psychoanalysis is, ideally, the exercise of the symbolic function (based on the structure of language and the subject's particular constitution by speech, symbolic interaction, family structure and relationships, etc.).

responseSet.title: Magic and the Aesthetic Illusion
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Magic and the Aesthetic Illusion

Leon Balter

Leon Balter

544 East 86th Street, 1 SW
New York, NY 10028
Lbaltermd@aol.com

Leon Balter  

The aesthetic illusion is the subjective experience that the content of a work of art is reality. It has an intrinsic relation to magic, an intrapsychic maneuver oriented toward modification and control of the extraspyschic world, principally through ego functioning. Magic is ontogenetically and culturally archaic, expresses the omnipotence inherent in primary narcissism, and operates according to the logic of the primary process. Magic is a constituent of all ego functioning, usually latent in later development. It may persist as an archaic feature or may be evoked regressively in global or circumscribed ways. It causes a general disinhibition of instincts and impulses attended by a sense of confidence, exhiliration, and exuberance. The aesthetic illusion is a combination of illusions: (1) that the daydream embodied by the work of art is the beholder's own, the artist being ignored, and (2) that the artistically described protagonist is a real person with a real “world.” The first illusion arises through the beholder's emotional-instinctual gratification from his or her own fantasy-memory constellations; the second comes about because the beholder, by taking the protagonist as proxy, mobilizes the subjective experience of the imaginary protagonist's “reality.” The first illusion is necessary for the second to take place; the second establishes the aesthetic illusion proper. Both illusions are instances of magic. Accordingly, the aesthetic illusion is accompanied by a heady experience of excitement and euphoria. The relation among the aesthetic illusion, magic, and enthusiasm is illustrated by an analytic case, J. D. Salinger's “The Laughing Man,” Woody Allen's Play It Again, Sam, Don Quixote, and the medieval Cult of the Saints.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Kohut's Freudian Vision

Philip F. D. Rubovits-Seitz in collaboration with Heinz Kohut. Hillsdale, NJ: The Analytic Press, 1999, xix + 234 pp., $49.95.

Paul Ornstein

Paul Ornstein

60 Longwood Avenue #509
Brookline, MA 02446
Fax: 617-738-9779
E-mail: Ornstep@email.uc.edu

Paul Ornstein  

Psychoanalysts and historians of psychoanalytic ideas are indebted to Philip Rubovits-Seitz for publishing his notes on Heinz Kohut's two-year theory course at the Chicago Institute for Psychoanalysis. Kohut reviewed these notes with Seitz, authenticating that they faithfully reflected what he conveyed in his lectures. These notes are of interest to me because I attended these lectures in 1960/61 and 1961/62 and

338

could, on reading the notes, almost hear Kohut's delivery. Thus was my reading informed by my experience of Kohut's intense immersion in, his profound grasp of, Freud's ideas. He spoke not about them but from within them, since he lived these ideas. That vivid memory reanimated for me this inescapably “bloodless,” however clear and accurate, condensation. Indeed, it must be said that the notes are fully understandable only by those already steeped in Freud's ideas. What the text does convey, clearly and compellingly, is how far our field has developed (even in the now mythical mainstream of the American Psychoanalytic Association) since Kohut taught Freudian theory.

The book is divided into four parts. Part I, “Kohut's Lectures on Psychoanalytic Psychology” (34 chapters) presents the notes taken by Rubovits-Seitz. Part II, “Concepts and Theories of Psychoanalysis,” reprints a synthesis of the lectures prepared by Kohut and Rubovits-Seitz in 1963 for inclusion in a textbook. Part III, “Kohut's Method of Synthesizing Freudian Theory,” is a detailed assessment of that subject. Finally, in Part IV Rubovits-Seitz presents his views of Kohut's thinking under the rubric “Kohut's Concepts of Narcissism and Self Psychology: Continuities with Freudian Theory.” I will comment briefly on each of the four parts and then discuss Rubovits-Seitz's views of Kohut's Freudian vision from my own current perspective.

Part I. The notes, meticulously formulated, focus on the key issues of each lecture and present a coherent view of Freud's contributions. These notes may serve knowledgeable young analysts, or candidates preparing for final examinations, as a reliable syllabus—a quick overview of how the various parts of the theory hang together (or are made to hang together) in this rendition. The notes do not point to anything drastically different from Freud's own formulations; neither do they hint at Kohut's growing dissatisfaction with the experience-distant nature of the theory and the ever widening gap between theory and practice he remarked on in the late 1960s. All that became evident only much later, but by then he was no longer teaching the course. When, after graduating in 1966, I approached Kohut for private supervision, I did not know he had developed ideas of his own, but had come to him for his exquisite, widely admired, and creative knowledge of Freud and for his notable clinical sophistication, which he employed with enviable ease. I learned about his new ideas only in supervision, where his emphasis was entirely on what was novel both in his thinking and in his evolving treatment principles.

[[RunningHead]]

The Feeling of What Happens: Body and Emotion in the Making of Consciousness

Antonio R. Damasio. New York: Harcourt Brace, 1999, 400 pp., $28.00 hardcover, $15.00 paperback.

Jeremy Mack

Jeremy Mack

10 East 96th Street
New York, NY 10128
Fax: 212-327-2436
E-mail: Jrm10e@aol.com

Jeremy Mack  

In The Feeling of What Happens, Antonio Damasio, a preeminent worker in the field of cognitive neuroscience, attempts to define consciousness in such a way as to expose its somatic roots and to return to a Darwinian view of the working of the brain. His is a passionate, as

356

well as reasoned, exposition of concepts which, not quite gettable in a linear way, require like Magic Cards the reader's constant staring and concentration until, with a snap, a card yields up its three-dimensional secret. Damasio's relentless message, here and in his previous book, Descartes' Error, is that emotion, consciousness, and brain coexist within, and are inseparable from, the same body.

Damasio is Allen Distinguished Professor and Head of the Department of Neurology at the University of Iowa College of Medicine in Iowa City, and Adjunct Professor at the Salk Institute. He is a prolific researcher, having written some 245 articles, including “A Neural Basis for Sociopathy” and “Fear and the Human Amygdala.” In his earlier book he elucidated the origin of feeling in the body and supported his view that the attempt to keep reason and emotion separate is fatally flawed. This was the view too of Gilbert Ryle (1949), who criticized the Cartesian dualistic view as a category error. Expressive of Damasio's amazingly wide erudition, which enables him to avoid dualism by seeing science in the service of humanism, his explanatory references extend across rock music, Beckett, Shakespeare, musical comedy, and Mozart. His apparent familiarity with ancient literature allows him to remind us that the Greek word for mind was psyche, which also denoted breath and blood.

In keeping with his romantic view, Damasio presents the story of consciousness as if it were a vast Wagnerian drama: organism contends with object, and the self, especially as it first appears in the guise of proto-self, emerges as hero. These struggles produce consciousness, which is our uniquely human condition of mind, characterized by the creation of a self so situated as to be able to monitor changes in the organism as it interacts with the objects that impinge on it. This self is felt as us.

[[RunningHead]]

Perversion and Utopia: A Study in Psychoanalysis and Critical Theory

Joel Whitebook. Cambridge: MIT Press, 1995, 350 pp., $52.00 hardcover, $24.00 paperback.

Arnold Goldberg

Arnold Goldberg

122 South Michigan Avenue
Chicago, IL 60603-6107
Fax: 312-922-6798
E-Mail: Docaig@aol.com

Arnold Goldberg  

Critical Theory is a social science theory associated with the Frankfurt School, a renowned institute for social research founded in 1923. The theory extends certain philosophical elements, as well as psychoanalysis, into Marxism. Its leading members were Max Horkheimer, Theodor Adorno, and Herbert Marcuse. During the Nazi period the school dispersed and eventually regrouped in New York around the New School for Social Research (now New School University). In 1949 it moved back to Frankfurt, where Jürgen Habermas emerged as its foremost figure. Although Critical Theory is concerned with culture and modernism, it is basically inspired by Hegel and is quite opposed to Soviet Marxism.

370

Therapeutic Action: A Guide to Psychoanalytic Therapy

Enrico E. Jones. Northvale, NJ: Jason Aronson, 2000, 386 pp., $60.00.

Allan Compton

Allan Compton

1081 Moraga Drive
Los Angeles, CA 90049
E-mail: Alacpr@ucla.edu

Allan Compton   Training and Supervising Analyst, Los Angeles Psychoanalytic Society and Institute; Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, UCLA; Founder and Director, Los Angeles Center for Psychoanalytic Research.

Enrico Jones's Therapeutic Action is a truly remarkable book. I became personally acquainted with the author at a meeting of the Society for Psychotherapy Research (SPR) held in Vancouver perhaps ten years ago. He was presenting a paper ostensibly about a teaching technique. In fact, however, he was reporting an attempt to deal with a facet of what is perhaps the most deleterious schism in our field. In his teaching experience he had observed that psychology graduate students tend, from the beginning of their programs, to be interested in careers either in research or in clinical work, but not in a combination of the two. If that trend is present from the start of graduate work, he reasoned, it must have originated earlier-at least as early as undergraduate days. As an experiment, he had his undergraduates study transcripts of dynamic psychotherapy sessions and evaluate them using several different formal methods for systematically studying psychotherapeutic process (e.g., Luborsky's CCRT and Dahl's FRAMES). If we look at Jones's course as an experiment exploring the possibility of arousing the interest of undergraduate psychology students in psychotherapy-oriented research, the results confirm the idea that the goal can be accomplished. Indeed, the results were manifest in the room that day, in the presence of twelve undergraduate psychology students who had taken the trouble to travel from San Francisco to Vancouver to attend the meeting.

In Therapeutic Action Jones is striving to deal with the same problem, or a similar problem at a more advanced level. The book is addressed first of all to psychotherapists. It is a how-to book on psychotherapy, well suited to those beginning this pursuit. In this aspect, it takes its place in a line of classic works on psychotherapeutic technique, such as those by Frieda Fromm-Reichmann, Sidney Tarachow, and, at a psychoanalytic level, Otto Fenichel and Charles Brenner. It is, however, thoroughly contemporary, in the best sense, in today's psychotherapeutic world.

Robert Tyson

5174 Chelsea Street

Robert Tyson   Training and Supervising Analyst (Adult and Child), San Diego Psychoanalytic Society and Institute; Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, University of California San Diego; Fellow, Royal College of Psychiatrists.

Rose Edgcumbe's outstanding book should prove immensely valuable to a wide audience—for clinicians in a position to evaluate and treat disturbed children, those interested in or teaching development in a psychoanalytic context, and adult therapists in search of a clinically based developmental framework within which to understand their patients. As Edgcumbe says, this isn't a biography of Anna Freud, who died in 1983. Rather, it is a concise developmental biography of a

689

significant and fertile stream of psychoanalytic theory and technique and their applications, a stream that originated with Anna Freud, an adult analyst who was among the first to explore the possibilities of psychoanalytic work with children. Anna Freud's frame of reference, predominantly within the evolving contemporary structural model (see, e.g., Sandler et al. 1997), survives translation into other perspectives remarkably well, should one be motivated to undertake it. Much of her work has been incorporated into the thinking and practice of psychoanalysts everywhere—though, it is true, in different proportions and not always with awareness of the source. Edgcumbe's book is particularly relevant today, when children the world over are subject to seriously deleterious, if not traumatic, influences on their development. As a guide to the thinking and work of the psychoanalyst who has made the most significant, practical, and comprehensive contributions to our understanding of the broad range of human development, it is unsurpassed.

The book is remarkable also for its successful integration of the many historical, political, and psychoanalytic constituents of that fertile stream, and for Edgcumbe's clear and eminently readable style, reminiscent of Anna Freud herself. However, when discussing reasons why Anna Freud's work is not better known, Edgcumbe does not consider her elegantly simple writing style as being paradoxically less interesting to many who equate obscurity with profundity. She does provide good explanations for why the work is better known in the United States (p. 200) but offers no reasons for Hamilton's finding (1996) that it was possible to complete psychoanalytic training in the British Psychoanalytical Society without reading anything by Anna Freud.

Consummated Mother-Son Incest in Latency

A Case Report of an Adult Analysis

Howard Rudominer

Howard Rudominer

59 Springbrook Road
Livingston, NJ 07039
Fax: 973-992-4449
E-mail: Hrudmd@yahoo.com

Howard Rudominer  

Reported cases of mother-son incest are very rare in the psychoanalytic literature; the fact of such incest, however, may not be so rare as has generally been believed. A detailed case report of the analysis of an adult with a history of severe physical, sexual, and verbal abuse, including consummated incest with his mother during latency, is considered in the context of other reported studies. The author raises some issues of resistance and countertransference that may influence the reporting, treatment, and perhaps even recognition of cases of mother-son incest.

[[RunningHead]]

Nocturnes: On Listening to Dreams

Paul Lippmann. Hillsdale, NJ: The Analytic Press, 2000, 264 pp., $45.00.

Irwin Rosen

Irwin Rosen

3506 SW Avalon Lane
Topeka, KS 66604

Irwin Rosen   Training and Supervising Analyst, Greater Kansas City Psychoanalytic Institute.

MEMO

To: Paul Lippmann, author

FROM: Irwin C. Rosen, reviewer

I think that one of the most important ideas you share in Nocturnes is that, upon hearing a dream, the listener is most helpful when he or she doesn't try to “nail it” (i.e., to tear off mentally in search of its correct meaning or its proper interpretation), but instead enters into a “conversation” with the dreamer—a relaxed, non-pursuing excursion in which the dream can reveal its own place and purpose in the dreamer's life.

Well, here I was, an unconscionably long time ago, faced with the weighty job of reviewing your remarkable book for the readers of this journal. And I proceeded to behave just as I thought a diligent reviewer should. I filled pages of a legal pad with notes. I highlighted and underlined and put exclamation points, question marks, and comments in the margins. And the more scrupulously I did all those reviewer things, the farther I felt myself departing from the points that you were making in the book and, more importantly, from its spirit.

Where you were beckoning toward a receptive, playful, “unfolding” approach to the dream, one that goes beyond evenly suspended attention, I, in my attempt to be a meticulous, knowing (and impressive) reviewer, was doing everything that you cautioned us not to be and do in listening to and responding to dreams. So I stepped out of the role of reviewer with its mavenish scrutinizing and hypervigilant attitude (like the one that too often characterizes our approach to dreams) and instead decided to “allow the book to happen to me” the way you suggest we approach dreams, and to “converse with you”—and, hopefully, with those who will read the book and these comments.

I also discovered that I was (as you urge dream-listeners to try not to do) sedulously pureeing your thoughts in the blender of my own theoretical system—a deeply valued, and I believe clinically effective, set of ideas formed during nearly fifty years of training and work at the Menninger Clinic. Shaping those ideas was a solid grounding in Rapaportian ego-psychology, expanded by Kernberg's object relations approach, Jock Sutherland's unique respect for the autonomous self, and a later infusion of attachment theory.

Psychoanalytic Explorations in Art

Ernst Kris. Madison, CT: International Universities Press, 2000, 358 pp., $29.95 paperback.

Laurie Wilson

Laurie Wilson

175 West 12th Street (Suite 6A)
New York, NY 10011-8214
Fax: 212-620-0292
E-mail: Ljwl@nyu.edu

Laurie Wilson  

On its publication in 1952, Ernst Kris's Psychoanalytic Explorations in Art was universally seen as having opened new territory in the study of art, moving beyond formulaic psychobiographical studies of artists to study form, style, artistic invention, and the communicative aspect of art. Most early reviews were adulatory, including the astute, acerbic review by David Rapaport (1954).

Some of the book's fourteen essays had been written and published in the thirties and grew out of Freud's conviction of the usefulness of applying analytic attitudes and insights to the phenomena of culture. Kris was convinced, in addition, that psychoanalysis had something to learn from art, artists, and the study of creativity. He added, though, that his book was not a systematic presentation of the psychoanalytic psychology of art—the time had not yet come for such a volume. Despite the vast range of his observations he leaves room for—and indeed he repeatedly invites—further exploration of the subject.

Though Kris's book is still considered an interdisciplinary classic, it is not a book for the layperson, and that unfortunately may include the average art historian. Too much knowledge and unconflicted familiarity with psychoanalytic observations is assumed. But it is a book without peer, then or now, with regard to art and psychoanalysis. Its numerous flashes of brilliant hypothesizing are based on a combination of clinical work and scholarly research in the humanities and social sciences. The book is never simple, and its author never sacrifices the complexity of his subject for a less nuanced idea or set of hypotheses. Unfortunately, the writing is sometimes thick with a variety of observations which, though cogent, distract the reader from the main points. But Kris's scholarly gifts and exceptional ability to attend to details and find their deeper meanings more than make up for this flaw.

1339

Defining Character Integrity

Mardi Horowitz

Mardi Horowitz

401 Parnassus Avenue
Box 0984 (Room 346)
San Francisco, CA 94143
E-mail: Mardi@itsa.ucsf.edu

Mardi Horowitz  

Previous work on the integrity of character is reviewed and a contemporary definition offered using concepts of subordinate and supraordinate schematizations of self and relationships with others. The definition is clarified by a developmental perspective on possible growth in character integrity over the life cycle. Three types are defined by patterns in sense of identity, object relations, and defensive control of emotion. Such type formulations are useful because technique can be varied according to the inferred level of character integrity.
