A History of Child Psychoanalysis

Claudine Geissmann and Pierre Geissmann. London: Routledge, 1998, xv + 352 pp., $105.00 hardcover, $41.95 paperback.

Kirby Pope

Kirby Pope

325 SW Frazier Avenue
Topeka, KS 66606
Fax: 785-232-8611
E-mail: Kpope2@cox.net

Kirby Pope   faculty, Greater Kansas City Psychoanalytic Institute.

Claudine and Pierre Geissmann have explored through the lens of their remarkable historical exegesis the vectors that have impacted and shaped the field of child psychoanalysis. The central importance of analytic work with children to the development of psychoanalytic thought and practice in general is convincingly demonstrated by a clear exposition of the theories, techniques, and working orientations of the major thinkers in the field and their followers, as well as by a focus on more neglected figures. The reader is ineluctably drawn to this conclusion, while at the same time forced to confront the ongoing resistance to the full acceptance of child analytic work as a genuine form of psychoanalysis. The roots of this resistance are linked historically by the Geissmanns to the fear-arousing concept of infantile sexuality, the role of women and children in society, the question of lay analysis, and a group resistance to understanding the true essence of the child. As the authors point out, analysts in their own analyses have often unconsciously desired to gain control over, rather than understand, the child within.

Even so, there has been a lively interest over time in what observing and working with children can tell us about theoretical problems in psychoanalysis in general. Sigmund Freud recognized the necessity of studying children, and the Wednesday evening sessions of the Psychoanalytic Society of Vienna served as an early forum for discussion in this area.

685

Changing Conceptions of Psychoanalysis: The Legacy of Merton M. Gill

Edited by Doris K. Silverman and David L. Wolitzky. Hillsdale, NJ: The Analytic Press, 2000, 328 pp., $36.00.

Sydney Pulver

Sydney Pulver

111 North 49th Street
Philadelphia, PA 19139

Sydney Pulver   Training and Supervising Analyst, Psychoanalytic Center of Philadelphia; Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, University of Pennsylvaniaa School of Medicine.

If anyone deserves a festschrift, it is Merton M. Gill. Gill blazed his irascible way through the jungles of ego psychology, leading the march into intersubjectivity and during the journey making outstanding contributions to our conceptions of transference, countertransference, one- versus two-person psychology, and the difference between psychoanalysis and psychotherapy. This book is unusual. True to its title, it talks about Gill personally and, in the course of doing so, discusses in fascinating detail the many controversies in which he was involved.

The book begins with five personal reminiscences, each of which has its own distinct flavor, some recounting professional contacts, others recalling personal encounters, and each conveying another aspect of Gill's personality. Within these reminiscences, Gill's participation in the rise and fall of ego psychology and the career of David Rapaport (his espousal and systemization of ego psychology, his acolytes-turned-apostates, and the heroic attempts to make ego psychology work and the disillusionment that ultimately led Gill to object relations and intersubjectivity) are all frankly and engagingly discussed. Irwin Hoffman, who knew him intimately and worked with him most extensively, contributes a beautiful survey of Gill's development and peregrinations through the land of psychoanalytic theory. And then the book moves to the controversies that Gill delighted in. Robert Holt blasts him for his hermeneutic leanings, Morris Eagle takes issue with some of his views on transference and countertransference, and Lawrence Friedman objects to his ultimate abandonment of objectivity.

715

This is an aptly titled, very moving little book written in journalistic style on a topic which, though vital to certain patients, is not often mentioned in the analytic literature. The author, a psychotherapist, tells her own story of growing up in the shadow of her disturbed brother Steven. Psychologically oriented vignettes are included from sixty other “normal” individuals who have survived life with a physically or emotionally damaged sibling. The author emphasizes that “normal siblings are inundated daily with behavior that is … noxious, frightening, incomprehensible and wretchedly sad. Pity and outrage intermingle, and anger implodes when the perpetrator is also the victim” (p. 73). She names this sibling experience the “Caliban syndrome,” but I think it should really be called the “Miranda syndrome,” after the victim rather than after the repugnant slave of Shakespeare's Tempest.

716

Phyllis Tyson Replies

Phyllis Tyson

Phyllis Tyson

Phyllis Tyson  

September 25, 2002. I thank Auchincloss and Vaughan for clarification of their position. I concur with their assertion that serious errors may occur, with dire consequences for our field's credibility, when developmental assumptions are made solely on the basis of consulting room data from adult patients. This is where child analysis can make a significant contribution. Children in analysis demonstrate a remarkable capacity for introspection when interpretations follow and integrate the free associations of their fantasy play. This allows the child analyst to observe the inner world of the child as it emerges. Of course, child analysts must beware of the opposite error: that is, assumptions about later outcome made on the basis of developmental data alone.

I appreciate Auchincloss and Vaughan's making clear that we share a conviction about psychoanalytic theory building. Credible evolution of theory can occur only when psychoanalytic observations from the consulting room are integrated with research data generated in all relevant scientific domains.

996

[[RunningHead]]

The Dove that Returns, the Dove that Vanishes: Paradox and Creativity in Psychoanalysis

Michael Parsons. London: Routledge, 2000, 224 pp., $67.95

Steven Cooper

Steven Cooper

875 Massachusetts Avenue, Suite 54
Cambridge, MA 02139
E-mail: Scoop13066@aol.com

Steven Cooper   Training and Supervising Analyst, Boston Psychoanalytic Society and Institute; Supervising Analyst, Massachusetts Institute for Psychoanalysis; Clinical Associate Professor of Psychology, Harvard Medical School.

Michael Parsons's The Dove That Returns, The Dove That Vanishes: Paradox and Creativity in Psychoanalysis, is a book of clarity and poignancy. It is a book that rings true both in the author's presentation of clinical material and in his formulations about clinical technique and process.

The book aims to explore conflicts and points of convergence between the intrinsically personal and subjective dimensions of psychoanalytic work and the need for technical discipline (which Parsons calls objectivity). He also explores the personal dimension of the psychoanalytic practitioner—the notion that the evolution of the analyst as a person and as a unique clinician is a precondition for creative analytic work.

The metaphors and methodology used in the book reflect Parsons's creativity and personality, and work synergistically to elucidate his interest in the analyst's personality. He is especially drawn to Chinese and Japanese martial arts as heuristic devices because they combine discipline and a requisite degree of skill with an opportunity for expressiveness—not just superficial or descriptive forms of expressiveness, but something that expresses deep, unbidden, and even sometimes unknown aspects of one's nature. This is a lovingly used, real, and helpful way to describe many of the tensions between spontaneity and restraint, between the artful exploration and vocation (as he comes to call them) that Parsons is most interested in pursuing with the reader.

The opening chapter, “Vocation and Martial Arts,” is a beautifully written exploration of the rich contradictions intrinsic to psychoanalytic work. Citing Rank and Lacan, Parsons explores how these theorists viewed analysis as a means to developing meaning.

1039

The Shame Response to Rejection

Herbert E. Thomas. Sewickley, PA: Albanel, 1997, 86 pp., $26.00.

James McLaughlin

James McLaughlin

220 N. Bellefield Avenue #403
Pittsburgh, PA 15213
Fax: 412-621-0940
E-mail: Jtm@telerama.com

James McLaughlin   Training and Supervising Analyst, Pittsburgh Psychoanalytic Institute; Associate Professor of Psychiatry Emeritus, University of Pittsburgh School of Medicine.

This small volume speaks to all of us who in any way—whether as judge or teacher, parent or analytic therapist—hold power over others, and thereby hold both the power to avoid or inflict rejection and shaming, and the responsibility to provide remediation.

Herbert Thomas as psychoanalyst provides us with a fresh perspective on matters of shame. He centers shame in the experience of rejection, at the moment when needed acceptance is repudiated in ways that reject the need and objectify the seeker. He finds this root of shame to be the major painful affect state driving our psychosocial development.

Thomas as physician wants us to grasp that this moment is one of unspeakable physical pain. He cites word fragments gleaned from patients, poets, and writers. Their gropings touch the nadir of human experience: body ego states of emptiness and crushing pain in chest and abdomen, total nothingness, fear and helpless rage—feelings akin to abandonment at its worst.

1088

Anna Aragno. Madison, CT: International Universities Press, 1997, 435 pp., $65.00.

Mary Target

Mary Target

Gower Street
London WCIE 6BT
United Kingdom
Fax: +44-171-916-8502

Mary Target  

Anna Aragno's book sets itself a highly ambitious goal, a new psychoanalytic model of mind that “operationalizes meaning” (p. 385). To discover the new model, I eagerly began reading. Unfortunately, the author's writing style, in the first two chapters especially, combines density and emphatic assertion, which made access to her ideas rather difficult. She adopts a proselytizing tone, which I, as an English nonconformist, found alienating.

I found myself carried along, but sometimes submerged, by a torrent of complex and portenously phrased sentences. A sentence quoted on the jacket is typical: “Our switch in epistemological gestalt opens the way for an experiential developmental model of subjective experience in which human symbolic systems, their stages of development and organisation, along the unconscious-conscious dimension, and their variegated modes and means of expression can begin to be understood” (p. 51). Faced with such sentences, I constantly wanted to translate them into simpler and no less exact language, as in the case above: “A new approach allows for a developmental model of subjective experience, showing the evolution of symbolization in the conscious and unconscious, with its varied forms of expression.” This style may have two particular origins. One is that the work was originally a Ph.D. dissertation, a genre of writing that requires an approach comprehensive yet at the same time focused on the author's development.

348

[[RunningHead]]

Letters

Cavell, Friedman, and Objectivity

Arnold Goldberg

Arnold Goldberg

122 South Michigan Avenue
Chicago, IL 60603-6107
Fax 312-922-6798

Arnold Goldberg  

May 10, 2002. Surely any untrained philosopher would be puzzled by the exchange of letters between Marcia Cavell and Lawrence Friedman (J. Amer. Psychoanal. Assn.50/1) in which they claim to agree with one another while (to me) seeming to do no such thing. Cavell tells us that one may feel certainty, but that it is merely subjective. One may also have knowledge that may be taken as true, but then this knowledge may turn out to be mistaken. We can assume, I would think, that these statements can be made about her own positive theses. Friedman tells us that both he and Cavell know that there is an object out there, while stating that what haunts philosophy is a thoroughly indeterminate world. We can probably assume that that worry certainly does not belong to Friedman. He has possibly forgotten that Cavell told us that his certitude is merely subjective.

I would like to disagree with the both of them. They belong to the group of thinkers much influenced by the analytic tradition of a spectator view of the world. This is a picture of someone looking out at and then representing (or characterizing) the world. In order to allow for one's personal beliefs coloring the world, they rely on some third point of view (Friedman, p. 327) to gain accuracy. This subtly introduces a “view from nowhere.” Alas, that is arguable if not dispensable. Rather we should recognize that we are always in the world, and we cannot disengage from it. Of course this is merely an alternative way to think about things, since I also can make no claim to certitude. I claim only that one's relating to the world makes true what we attribute to the world.

Think of it this way. There is no single way the world is. That is not to say that we invent the world or that it exists only in our minds. It is to say that our ways of seeing things often outstrip our language for describing them, and that the same words can describe the world in entirely different ways. Davidson, much quoted in the above exchange, writes: “The truth of an utterance depends on just two things: what the words mean, and how the world is arranged.” For a trenchant criticism of Davidson, I recommend the philosopher Charles Travis, who writes: “In Davidson's view, there is a way words are to be understood to

Cordelia Schmidt-Hellerau  

August 4, 2001. The presentation and discussion of Jay Greenberg's“The Analyst's Participation: A New Look” (J. Amer. Psychoanal. Assn.49/2) is a very interesting and important opening to critically reflect on and “identify psychoanalytic excess” (p. 360) in the relational branch of our field. Although Greenberg emphasizes his previous dismissal of classical psychoanalytic positions, which seem to him “based on an outmoded philosophical foundation” (p. 368), he is also pondering the short'comings of relational practice. His acknowledgment of the analyst's “excess of wanting to make something happen” (p. 378), and his warning, “something has gotten lost,” remind me of Goethe's poem “The Sorcerer's Apprentice”: “Spirits raised by me / vainly would I lay.

Schwaber on Meissner and Psychic Reality

Evelyne Schwaber

Evelyne Schwaber

4 Welland Road
Brookline, MA 02445
Fax: 617-632-8968

Evelyne Schwaber  

November 18, 2001. In W. W. Meissner's paper, “Psychic Reality in the Psychoanalytic Process” (J. Amer. Psychoanal. Assn.49/3), he notes that there are objective observational data that one can gather, as in reading a text. Meissner states, “Our consensus regarding the reality of the words can be ruffled by my misreading—I might read “bias” for “basis” —but the distortion can be corrected by returning to the text and rereading it. That much is objective.… The text is objective and my reading can be double-checked so that my knowing it is objective” (pp. 856-857). Accordingly, I would like to return to the text of some of my published statements in apposition to Meissner's rendering of my views.

He writes: “Validation of the analyst's psychic reality has come under attack from theorists insisting on the primacy of the patient's psychic reality…, as though the one were inherently opposed to the other—if one was valid, the other could not be” (p. 861). He cites me as a proponent of this position, stating that I make “appeals for uncritical acceptance and validation of the patient's rendering” (p. 865), and attempt “to reduce or suspend the role of the analyst's own subjective experience” (p. 868). In disagreement with my presumed stance, he observes, “the analyst's psychic reality … can even be heuristically useful, or necessary, when a discrepancy arises between the patient's version and his own” (p. 861). Citing the conceptual question I had posed—“To whose point of view do we refer?”—he responds, “The answer for me is that neither view is disqualified and neither is privileged, but both are open to questioning and exploration. But to distinguish my approach from Schwaber's, I do not find it possible to disqualify my own experience and awareness in favor of the patient” (pp. 877-878).

To revisit the text in which I posited this question (“From Whose Point of View?”Psychoanal. Q., 1998)

[[RunningHead]]

Hidden Faults

Steven A. Frankel. Madison, CT: Psychosocial Press, 2000, xiv + 212 pp., $35.00

Joyce Slochower

Joyce Slochower

15 West 75th Street
New York, NY 10023
E-mail: Slochowr@aol.com

Joyce Slochower   Faculty and Supervising Analyst, Psychoanalytic Institute of Northern California; Professor of Psychology, City University of New York.

Hidden Faults, by Steven A. Frankel, is an accessible, highly readable text that explicates the author's personal journey as a psychoanalyst. It focuses on the nature and repair of therapeutic disjunctions—that is, “failures in interpersonal engagement that foreclose understanding” (p. xii). Frankel explores the therapist's effort to study and work with these ruptures, emphasizing that disjunctions, although inevitable, must be repaired if the therapy is to proceed in a useful way. Indeed, he argues that their resolution is crucial to the change process. The bulk of the book details Frankel's engagement with a number of difficult patients, describing the various clinical shapes that disjunctions can take and illustrating both his and his patients' efforts to resolve them. His vivid vignettes disclose his own contributions to disjunction as he carefully tracks the evolution of difficult therapeutic interchanges that can freeze or derail the treatment.

Frankel strongly emphasizes the collaborative therapeutic relationship, although he acknowledges that “the most seriously disturbed patients need to sustain blatant illusions” (p. 136) and may use disjunctions as organizing tools. Yet from my perspective, ongoing disjunctions sometimes represent crucial reenactments of early dissociated object experiences, or desperate yet necessary attempts to ward off affective contact or intimacy. In these instances, the therapist's attunement and attempts to repair disjunctions may excessively threaten the patient's experience of emotional autonomy. Frankel could have addressed more fully those times when patients need to experience therapist or process as unhelpful—or even toxic—for long periods of time.

Working within a relational framework, Frankel views affiliation as a primary motivation, and he emphasizes the need of both therapist and patient eventually to restore rapport by seeking attunement. His main theoretical concept is of a self and object (SO)

[[RunningHead]]

What I Heard in the Silence: Role Reversal, Trauma, and Creativity in the Lives of Women

Maria V. Bergmann. Madison, CT: International Universities Press, 2000, xiii + 211 pp., $29.95 paperback

Nancy Kulish

Nancy Kulish

625 Purdy Street
Birmingham, MI 48009
E-mail: Nkulish@aol.com
Fax: 248-258-9348

Nancy Kulish  

At first glance this book appears to be a collection of essays on a variety of clinical subjects. While each of the ten chapters can in fact stand alone, they are unified by some common themes: what the author proposes as a frequently found dynamic of role reversal in her female patients; the centrality of the oedipal complex; the importance of bisexuality; and the recurring effects of trauma throughout the life cycle. Reading Maria Bergmann's rich clinical material, we become witness to her patients' attempts to cope with trauma, to make changes in the course of treatment, and to integrate these changes creatively in their lives. We are witness also to a parallel process within the author. Here we see a senior analyst at work: sharing her experience over many years, demonstrating how a traditionally trained Freudian analyst has integrated the many changes in psychoanalytic theory and technique into her current thinking and practice.

In the first chapter, Bergmann lays down the central dynamic of many of her cases, which will appear in the chapters to follow: mother-daughter role reversal, a crucial “genetic turning point” in her patients. During their childhoods with a narcissistically disturbed mother, the patients each took care of their mother's needs. This role reversal was accompanied by unfulfilled yearnings for mothering, unconscious wishes for symbiotic relationships, strong negative oedipal desires, and defenses against fears of reengulfment and abandonment. Adding to the picture were precociously sexualized relationships with their fathers and increased wishes for care from them in compensation for insufficient care from their mothers. Bisexual fantasies, gender confusion, and unresolved oedipal issues hampered further relationships. Here and throughout the book Bergmann clearly emphasizes her belief in the role of the parents in the pathology of their children.

In the second chapter these ideas are elaborated with the idea of the “dead” mother. In the internal world of these patients there is a good mother and a “dead” mother. This imago of the dead mother reflects the experience of being with a mother whose depression stems not from actual bereavement, as in the patients described by André Green, but from narcissistic disturbances.

[[RunningHead]]

The Course of Gay and Lesbian Lives: Social and Psychoanalytic Perspectives

Bertram J. Cohler and Robert M. Galatzer-Levy. Chicago: University of Chicago Press, 2000, 537 pp., $50.00.

John Ross

John Ross

50 East 78th Street
Suite 1C
New York, NY 10021
Fax: 212-249-9528
E-mail: LMLRoss@aol.com

John Ross   Training and Supervising Analyst, Columbia University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research; Clinical Professor, Department of Psychiatry, College of Physicians and Surgeons, Columbia University.

The Course of Gay and Lesbian Lives: Social and Psychoanalytic Perspectives contains treasure troves of information for the working psychoanalytic clinician. The problem is, they are often hard to find in this archipelago of a book.

With a mandate from the American Psychoanalytic Association to provide a state-of-the-art report on the extant research-developmental, biological, social, historical-on the contemporary gay and lesbian experience, the distinguished interdisciplinarian Bertram Cohler and his collaborator Robert Galatzer-Levy have produced an encyclopedic volume on this complex subject. Presenting evidence throughout the book to support the thesis that sexual orientation and psychopathology are in no way correlated, Cohler and Galatzer-Levy offer detailed reviews of the extensive literature on the subject, which has proliferated in the wake of the 1969 Stonewall Riot.

Mostly these critiques try to tell us what homosexuality is not. Thus, the authors

found that nonnormative expression of sexual desire and same-gender sexual orientation has little relationship to adjustment or experience of personal distress. Social theory and study of the lives of gay and straight men and women converge to show that the experience of sexual desire is not fixed but varies across the course of life. Sexual orientation should not be viewed from an essentialist perspective that regards sexual desire as predetermined by either innate or developmental factors; sexual desire is fluid and changing in its significance for society and persons over historical time and in lived experience within lifetimes. Little is known about factors leading to either heterosexuality or homosexuality. The meaning of same-gender desire is founded in social and historical circumstances which change over time and across generations or cohorts. Social contexts and personal life circumstances alike influence the presently told life story narrated and collaboratively reconstructed in psychoanalysis … [p. 423].

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Rhymes and Animals

Aspects of the Development of Play

John Rosegrant

John Rosegrant

441 West End Avenue
Suite IG
New York, NY 10024
E-mail: rosegrantj@msn.com

John Rosegrant  

The Lore and Language of Schoolchildren. By Iona Opie and Peter Opie. New York: New York Review Books, 2001 (originally published in 1959), 417 pp., $16.95.

Why the Wild Things Are: Animals in the Lives of Children. By Gail F. Melson. Cambridge: Harvard University Press, 2001, 236 pp., $27.95.

Psychoanalysis has two basic models for how play develops. Freud (1908) described play as continuing in the adult in the form of daydreaming, which retains the wish-fulfilling, pleasure-seeking, and unrealistic qualities of play, but without the link to action in the world. He further described creativity in writers as being like daydreaming that is shaped so that it is pleasing to the group, not merely to the day-dreamer. Winnicott (1953, 1967) gave us the idea that play develops out of early transitional experience, and that play then evolves into later forms of transitional experience—art, religion, and culture, the entire inherited tradition into which one is born. Thus, Freud directs our attention to the relationships between pleasure and reality, and between thought and action, as play develops, and Winnicott directs our attention to self-experience and the differentiation/integration between inner and outer reality; both indicate that later transformations of play are increasingly an experience shared with the social group.

The Lore and Language of Schoolchildren, by Iona and Peter Opie, is a treasure trove of naturalistic observations of children that can help us think about the development of play. The Opies were folklorists who collected all sorts of oral lore from British schoolchildren, ages six to fourteen, and published it in 1959; their book has now been reissued.

1321

[[RunningHead]]

The Power of the Inner Judge: Psychodynamic Treatment of the Severe Neuroses.

Leon Wurmser. Northvale, NJ: Jason Aronson, 2000, 368 pp., $60.00.

Howard Covitz

Howard Covitz

24 Latham Parkway
Melrose Park, PA 19027-3148
E-mail: Hhcovitz@aol.com

Howard Covitz   Institute for Psychoanalytic Psychotherapies, Melrose Park, PA.

Thirty years ago, the reach of psychoanalytic inquiry expanded exponentially, with a profusion of theories about certain especially problematic analysands who were coming to analysts in increasing numbers. Analytically trained practitioners, steeped as they were in Freud's theory and treatment of the neuroses, were confronted by transferences and transference-like relationships that were chaotic, that often were impervious to interpretive methods, and that not infrequently were accompanied by a dramatic exacerbation of symptoms. While borderline and narcissistic cases had been discussed by Michael Balint, Helene Deutsch, R. P. Knight, Annie Reich, and Adolf Stern, among others, the well-known writings of Kernberg, Kohut, Meissner, Searles, and a host of others presented clinical findings and offered new transference paradigms and corresponding technical recommendations with a clarity sufficient to foster the view that these patients, compared to their neurotic cousins, were sculpted from different stone or, at least, painted with a fundamentally different brush. And if these patients were to be considered sui generis, then how more appropriate a way to mark this distinction than by taxonomically situating such cases in their own well-circumscribed four cubits and by labeling those areas Borderline and Narcissistic Territory. Although in the Schreber case Freud (1911), restricted the confusion of of word and thing representations to schizophrenia, experience tells us that such naming processes as occurred in the writings on borderline and narcissistic disorders are not without influence over our perceptions. And so it was to be: borderlines and narcissists required their own very special brand of analytically informed therapy. And that was that—except for a few such as Peter Giovacchini (1979)

703

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Auchincloss and Vaughan on Tyson Commentary

Elizabeth Auchincloss and Susan Vaughan

Susan Vaughan

15 West 81st Street
New York, NY 10024
E-mail: Elauchin@mail.med.cornell.edu

25 West 81st Street, #1C
New York, NY 10024
E-mail: Scvl@Columbia.edu

Susan Vaughan  

July 14, 2002. We were surprised to find ourselves presented in the introduction to Phyllis Tyson's “Challenges of Psychoanalytic Developmental Theory” (J. Amer. Psychoanal. Assn.50/1) as paradigmatic of such “purists” as André Green, who has argued that excessive attention to empirical research is damaging to psychoanalysis, and Peter Wolff, who has written about “the irrelevance of infant observation for psychoanalysis” (J. Amer. Psychoanal. Assn.44/1). The central argument in our recent paper, “Psychoanalysis and Homosexuality: Do We Need a New Theory?” (J. Amer. Psychoanal. Assn.49/4), to which Tyson referred, is that when psychoanalysis has tried to go it alone in the business of theory making, the results have often been disastrous. This has been so not only when analysts have attempted to create developmental theories about the etiology of homosexuality, but also in both psychoanalytic theories of severe mental illness and in early theories about female sexuality. As we make clear in our paper, it was only when challenged with data from other disciplines that psychoanalysis was forced to confront shortcomings in these theories.

We also argue that confusion between the developmental point of view and the genetic point of view has created a major blind spot in psychoanalytic theory making about homosexuality. These points of view were most clearly (and we think accurately) delineated by Hartmann in 1951. The psychoanalytic clinical setting does include a developmental point of view inasmuch as analysts do directly observe the development of adults over time, and child analysts certainly do observe the development of children over time. However, for the most part, psychoanalysts have direct access to observations made only from the genetic point of view. When we assert that the developmental point of view, in contrast to the genetic, is not psychoanalytic, we mean that it is not (again in contrast to the genetic) dependent upon a method of observation unique to psychoanalysis-i.e., that of shared introspection and free association in the clinical setting. We certainly do not mean that a developmental point of view (which depends upon the direct observation of children) cannot be psychoanalytically informed or that it should not be of interest to psychoanalysts as they create theories or treat patients.

[[RunningHead]]

The Dream Interpreters

Howard Shevrin. The Psychoanalytic Connection and the Internet Press for Psychoanalysis: www.psychoanalysis.net/ippsa/titlepage.html,2001, 327 pp., no charge.

Sandra Walker

Sandra Walker

1120 Cherry Street, Suite 240
Seattle, WA 98104
Fax: 206-624-1399
E-mail: Walkersc@u.washington.edu

Sandra Walker   Clinical Associate, Seattle Psychoanalytic Society and Institute; Clinical Assistant Professor, Department of Psychiatry and Behavioral Sciences, University of Washington.

Howard Shevrin presents us with a novel that is unusual in three ways. It is freely available on the Internet. It is a 327-page free-verse poem. It is entirely about psychoanalysis. So, is it something more than a curiosity?

1026

Shevrin says that he has written a novel with psychoanalysis itself as the protagonist, a novel in which the inner pulse of analytic work reveals itself. Almost all of the action is confined to the consulting room. Almost all of the characters interact directly with only one other, patient or analyst, in a succession of psychoanalytic hours. Shevrin believes that, like ancient Greek theater, psychoanalytic work intensifies human drama and reveals the essences of human character and experience.

Verse, says Shevrin, captures the paradoxes of psychoanalysis: emotionally demanding emotional distance, one-sided but mutual emotional risk, and metaphor in ordinary speech. He sees these paradoxes as part of a rhythm that arises in the work from repetition within the living psychoanalytic form. The strict form and the repetition create choruses and refrains.

Shevrin hopes that in The Dream Interpreters he has conveyed the limitations of psychoanalytic work. He says that he wants readers to see the dual nature of psychoanalysis as art and science, and to understand how hard it is to become a psychoanalyst and to practice psychoanalysis. This is a tall order. Does Shevrin deliver the goods?

The Dream Interpreters is set in late 1950s Tennessee at a psychiatric institution that combines psychoanalytic work and training with outpatient and inpatient treatment and psychiatric research. The cast of characters unfolds, two at a time, as part of an enmeshed network. Frances Dreyfus, whose psychoanalytic-trainee husband Paul is analyzing Emily Patterson while in a training analysis with Foucault, is in analysis with Victor Kleinman who is himself in analysis with Kurt Denkman while his trainee wife Marlena is in a training analysis with Bernard Freeman. Denkman's wife Greta is analyzing Serey Potmose who is married to a psychiatrist, Michael Potmose. Only trainee Donald Prescott (although he is married) and his training analyst Paula Veroff seem somewhat removed from the web of interlocking dyads. An undercurrent of struggle for power runs through these pairs. It takes the form of contention around who will be named as the institution's new research director.

Cherishment: A Psychology of the Heart

Elizabeth Young-Bruehl and Faith Bethelard. New York: The Free Press, 2000, 268 pp., $24.00

Howard Covitz

Howard Covitz

24 Latham Parkway
Melrose Park, PA 19027-3148
E-mail: Hhcovitz@aol.com

Howard Covitz   Director, Institute of Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy, Philadelphia.

Two-thirds of a century ago, in a polemic against religion and theoretical anarchism, Freud (1933, p. 158) rhetorically queried: “Does psychoanalysis lead to a certain Weltanschauung?” Answering this question in the negative, he counterpoised empirical science against illusion and emotion. Science, in Freud's way of thinking, was about capturing the truth or, at least, about approximating closely towards the truth, while illusion was of and about the magical fulfillment of the wishes of childhood. However, while in the laboratory sciences it may be reasonable to assume that researches are independent of such loosely defined constructs as value and meaning, and of notions of good and evil or right and wrong, in the behavioral sciences such theoretical neatness may be a luxury at best.

1077

[[RunningHead]]

Oscar Wilde in the 1990S: The Critic as Creator.

Melissa Knox. Rochester, NY: Camden House, 2001, 220 pp., $65.00

Susan Vaughan

Susan Vaughan

25 West 81st Street, Suite IC
New York, NY 10024
Fax: 212-721-0408

Susan Vaughan  

Oscar Wilde once defined “the highest criticism” as “the record of one's own soul,” and insisted that only by “intensifying his own personality” could the critic interpret the personality and work of others.” In Oscar Wilde in the 1990s: The Critic as Creator, Melissa Knox examines the spate of literary criticism that over the past decade has been devoted to Wilde's life and work. She focuses particularly on the resistance in much of this work to biographical and psychological understanding, but also on instances of its successful use.

Applying Wilde's standards to his critics, she finds that the best of them take to heart his idea that the aim of criticism is “to see the object as in itself it really is not.” One hundred years after Wilde's rise and catastrophic fall, Knox argues that the best and most useful Wilde criticism is written by those who embrace biography rather than a more Foucauldian “death of the author” or “author as text” perspective. Wilde's appreciation for Walter Pater's notion that everyone sees through a “thick wall of personality” makes him a prophet of sorts for Freud, who was his exact contemporary.

In reviewing this recent body of criticism, Knox examines critics speaking from a variety of perspectives: traditional literary criticism; the new historicism; gay, queer, and gender criticism; reader response criticism; Irish ethnic studies and cultural criticism; and biographic studies. The section on gay, queer, and gender criticism focuses on an interesting debate, one familiar to psychoanalysts, between “constructionists,” who regard cultures and societies as the authors of forms of sexuality, as well as of the labels assigned them, and “essentialists” who take certain forms of sexuality to be innate or natural expressions of emotional, physiological, and biological states.

1383

[[RunningHead]]

Freud and Psychoanalysis.

W. W. Meissner, S.J. Notre Dame, IN: University of Notre Dame Press, 2000, xv + 279 pp., $20.00 paperback

Harriet Basseches

Harriet Basseches

2301 Connecticut Avenue, NW
Washington DC 20008-1730
Fax: 202-462-5710
E-mail: Hibasseches@cs.com

Harriet Basseches  

This ambitious compendium takes the reader on a journey both historical and topical through Freud's development of psychoanalysis. Along the way it gives a glimpse of ideas from some of Freud's coworkers and certain extenders of his ideas, as well as noting others who have used Freudian thought as a segue to their own divergent paths toward psychoanalytic synthesis. The word compendium captures the thoroughness of this effort, in that it designates a work that gathers and succinctly presents all the details essential to the comprehensive knowledge of a subject. W. W. Meissner's new book does just that, for the current state of psychoanalytic theory.

The book is directed explicitly toward informing “the potential student of general psychiatry about this fundamental discipline within psychiatry” (p. 1). Despite that expressed focus, however, there is nothing in the remainder of the book that would discourage readers from disciplines other than psychiatry. In fact, it would seem that the book is meant to interest students not only of mental health but of philosophy and religion as well. In short, anyone interested in the field may benefit from this systematic account of Freud's psychoanalytic thought and selective presentation of later theorists.

The organization of the book requires mention. Any selection from as vast an oeuvre as Freud's is a challenging task and is inevitably subject to questions about sequencing, emphases, and omissions. Certain points of view will be highlighted, while others are passed over quickly or ignored altogether. This is especially true when the aim is to include not only Freud's work but that of others in a burgeoning field. In acknowledgment of that constraint, the book is thoughtfully arranged, each chapter opening with general statements of the material to be discussed, followed by detailed explication with appropriate subheads to guide the reader. The text initially moves between an historical and a topical focus, starting with Freud's 1895 Project. An extensive discussion of the Project is followed by a chapter on the beginnings of psychoanalysis—Freud's work with Breuer and their Studies on Hysteria.

19 East 88th Street, #1D
New York, NY 10128
E-mail: EveCaligor@aol.com

Eve Caligor  

In his introductory remarks, Robert Glick pointed out that the title of the panel, “Meaning in Clinical Analysis: Created or Discovered?” touches on central questions about the nature and goals of psychoanalysis: What is the analyst's job? What knowledge or authority is required to do that job? And to what end? He commented that for most of the history of psychoanalysis these questions would have been viewed as either meaningless or subversive. Freud believed he was discovering what was in the patient's mind and was uncovering the patient's unconscious history and mental contents. For Freud, the possibility of the analyst's creating something in the patient would smack of suggestion and would jeopardize the positivist scientific structure of psychoanalysis. Glick pointed out that in contrast to Freud, Ferenczi believed that psychoanalysis created new meanings. For Ferenczi, it was the meaningfulness of the here and now, of lived analytic experience, that corrected the past. Ferenczi viewed analyst and patient as creating something together that changed the patient and allowed for insight, rather than viewing change as following insight. Glick raised the possibility that the therapeutic action of psychoanalysis may lie in the shared process of negotiating meanings and pursuing meaningfulness.

Glick suggested that the history of clinical psychoanalysis and its theoretical assumptions may be viewed as a series of answers to the questions posed to the panelists. These questions have been asked before. At the 1994 winter meetings in Philadelphia, Warren Poland, Arnold Goldberg, Charles Hanly, and Henry Smith addressed them in a panel titled “Meaning in the Clinical Moment.

Panel held at the Spring Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, New Orleans. May 2001.

Panelists: Robert A. Glick (chair), Henry Friedman, Jay Greenberg, Arnold Goldberg, Theodore Shapiro.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Hartmann Era

Edited by Martin S. Bergmann. New York: Other Press, 2000, 373 pp., $60.00.

Charles Brenner

Charles Brenner

1040 Park Avenue
New York, NY 10028
Fax: 212-734-2499
E-mail: Ecbrenner@surfree.com

Charles Brenner  

This book is the report of a two-day meeting sponsored by the Psychoanalytic Research and Development Fund of New York. It consists of two parts, a series of presented papers and a stenographic report of the ensuing discussion, the latter undoubtedly edited for publication. The principal paper, by editor Martin Bergmann, is “The Hartmann Era and Its Contribution to Psychoanalytic Technique.” This contribution takes as its central thesis that there “was a time in the United States when [Heinz] Hartmann was regarded as the undisputed leader of American psychoanalysis” (p. 1). Associated with him and more or less united in their theoretical outlook, according to Bergmann, were a number of coworkers, “including Ernst Kris, Rudolf Loewenstein, Edith Jacobson, Margaret Mahler, Annie Reich, Kurt Eissler, Max Schur, and David Rapaport, to name only those who were most influential” (p. 6). It seems to me that this is too parochial a view, one focused exclusively on New York and, even there, solely on a single group. There is no doubt that Hartmann was highly respected and much admired in the New York Psychoanalytic Institute; to call him the undisputed leader of American psychoanalysis, however, seems both to overvalue his position and to neglect an important aspect of our field even then, namely, the diversity of theoretical and technical viewpoints contained within it.

Despite the book's overestimation of Hartmann's importance, much of it is devoted to criticism of his aims and theories. Often the contributors who criticize him do so more harshly than seems necessary and not always quite fairly. One would not learn from this volume, in which Hartmann is most often portrayed as a theorist uninterested in clinical matters, that he was responsible for establishing the Treatment Center of the New York Psychoanalytic Institute and served for two years as its director. If one of his associates, David Beres, is to be believed, Hartmann was in fact a shrewd clinician.

[[RunningHead]]

Art and Creativity

The Survival of Images: Art Historians, Psychoanalysts, and the Ancients. By Louis Rose. Detroit: Wayne State University Press, 2001, 209 pp., $34.95.

Elsa Blum

Elsa Blum

23 The Hemlocks
Roslyn, NY 11576
Fax: 516-621-3014
E-mail: Elsablum@cs.com

Elsa Blum  

The slimness of this volume belies the wealth of information it contains, as author Louis Rose interweaves the rich ideas of Freud with those of several notable art historians of the early twentieth century. He focuses on the period beginning with the decade before World War I and ending in the years immediately after World War II. The author addresses a variety of issues important to those interested in art criticism, cultural history, and psychoanalysis, tracing them in the works of Freud, Ernst Kris, Aby Warburg, Emanuel Loewy, Fritz Saxl, and Ernst Gombrich. The scholarly character of the volume is notable; 145 pages of text are followed by 39 pages of chapter notes and bibliography. Rose himself refers to the book as an extended essay, and indeed each of its five chapters is essentially an essay in itself. The chapters are titled “Image-Making and Movement,” “Mental Pictures and Visual Art: Aby Warburg and Emanuel Loewy,” “Reenactments: Sigmund Freud,” “Rhythms of Renewal: Ernst Kris and Fritz Saxl,” and “A Memory of Greece.” Thought-provoking and replete with historical facts, the book is densely written and makes considerable demands on the reader. Although at times its main themes are obscured by myriad details, the effort expended in reading this stimulating book can be quite rewarding.

Chapter 1, “Image-Making and Movement,” explores the creation of the illusion of movement in artistic and mental images. Rose describes the consideration at the turn of the century of links between artists' individual creations and cultural products such as myth, ritual, and theater. The author notes that the writers he discusses explored how the artistic images “preserved the remnants of enactments and dramatizations from the past,” using the term mimesis to refer to “the survival and regeneration of those dramatic fragments” (p. 18).

The process by which Gustave Flaubert created the character of Emma Bovary is examined, as are various of the author's sources for the heroine and their transformation in the course of composing the novel. Certain aspects of the author's psychic makeup, including his bisexuality, are discussed in this light, as are Flaubert's early traumatic losses and their influence on his way of working. Finally, it is suggested that writing had multiple functions for the author and that the creation of Emma Bovary served as a partial solution to unmet needs

[[RunningHead]]

Catching the Ball

The Role of Play in Psychoanalytic Treatment

Cynthia Rose

Cynthia Rose

1829 Alamo Avenue
Colorado Springs, CO 80907
Fax: 719-475-7175
E-mail: crosepearl@aol.com

Cynthia Rose  

Paula Bernstein began by framing the panel: through the lens of “play” it would explore interactive processes currently at the forefront of psychoanalytic theory and clinical discussion. It was Freud who chose the metaphor of play to explain psychoanalytic process to physicians seeking to learn psychoanalytic technique. “We render the compulsion [to repeat instead of remember] harmless, and indeed useful, by giving it the right to assert itself in a definite field. We admit it into the transference as to a playground, in which it is allowed to expand in almost complete freedom and … to display to us everything in the way of pathogenic instincts that is hidden in the patient's mind” (1914, p. 154). Beres and Arlow (1974) added that “a measure of the analyst's empathic capacity lies in his ability to be stimulated by the patient's unconscious fantasy when the analyst himself is not yet aware of the existence or the nature of the patient's unconscious fantasy” (p. 45); Arlow (1979) wrote that “psychoanalysis is essentially a metaphorical enterprise” (p. 373).

Bernstein emphasized that the analytic pair communicate by way of metaphors delivered and received both consciously and unconsciously. It was Winnicott (1971), the pediatrician analyst, who described a transitional space in which meaning is reciprocally created. “Psychotherapy,” he said, “takes place in the overlap of two areas of playing, that of the patient and that of the therapist…. Where playing is not possible then the work done by the therapist is directed towards bringing the patient from a state of not being able to play into a state of being able to play” (p. 38).

Panel held at the Spring Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, New Orleans, May 6, 2001.

Panelists: Paula Bernstein (chair), Roy Aruffo, Judith Yanof, Glenn O. Gabbard. Discussant: Morris Peltz.

responseSet.title: Psychoanalysis in the Culture
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis in the Culture

Warren Poland

Warren Poland

Warren Poland  

And ye shall hallow the fiftieth year … it shall be a jubilee unto you.

—Leviticus 25:10

If psychoanalysis has a birth year, it is fair to think it 1897, the year that Freud made a world-shaking revolutionary breakthrough in his self-analysis. It was then that he carried Socrates' injunction to know oneself farther than any before had, working incisively beyond childhood fantasies of innocent victimhood to recognize his own forbidden urges and desires, those that lay behind his terrors. Regarding his earlier misconceptions, he then wrote Fliess, “I shall not tell it in Gath, or publish it in the streets of Askalon, in the land of the Philistines—but between ourselves I have a feeling more of triumph than of defeat (which cannot be right)” (Freud 1954, p. 17).1

Science and humanism both require knowledge to be shared in order for civilization to move forward. Fortunately, Freud did tell of what he learned and did originate a tradition of psychoanalytic publication. In the years since Freud, our literature has grown through open discourse, with observations and ideas shared, criticized, altered, and extended. For half a century, the Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association has been at the forefront of facilitating that

1Applied analysis, one of the themes of this issue, is as cogent regarding the onset of psychoanalysis as it is for contemporary times. The personal theme Freud exposed, insight into his own patricidal urges, was still evident in Freud's associations. The self-analytic breakthrough described was presented as a victory over defensive misunderstanding, but it was at the same time a continuing oedipal victory and, apparently, one about which Freud remained uncomfortable. His biblical association, that referring to Gath, etc., came from an oedipal victor who expressed grief over the loss of the father. When told of King Saul's death, David did not exult over his own accession but instead mourned, ordering that news of the king's death not be published to the nation's enemies. As Freud gained insight into his own buried hostility to his father, he spoke manifestly of shame over his errors, but with associations that exposed that as a displacement from conflict over oedipal victory.

[[RunningHead]]

Marcia Cavell Responds

Marcia Cavell

Marcia Cavell

1570 Olympus Avenue
Berkeley, CA 94708
E-mail: Cavell@uclink4.berkeley.edu

Marcia Cavell  

June 15, 2002. Arnold Goldberg seems to equate knowledge with certainty. (This equation is one of the things philosophers mean by “foundationalism.”) For a discussion and criticism of the idea I refer

1313

Goldberg to my “Knowledge, Consensus, and Uncertainty” (Int. J. Psycho-Anal.80/6, 1999). The arguments I present there, I should say, are by no means original with me, but are by now fairly standard among philosophers.

With some obvious annoyance, Goldberg says, “Cavell tells us that one may have.… knowledge that may be taken as true, but then this knowledge may turn out to be mistaken. We can assume, I would think, that these statements can be made of her own positive theses.” Certainly. I do not know why he thinks that either Lawrence Friedman or I are influenced by “a spectator view of the world,” and in any case would like to know what he means by this catchphrase. Of course we are always in the world. But does it follow that “one's relating to the world makes true what we attribute to the world”? Consider the following: “AIDS is caused by evil thoughts”; “Schizophrenia is a disease peculiar to white males”; “There are unicorns in the garden”; “Mary's wishes were responsible for the destruction of the World Trade Towers”; “On June 15, 2002, the World Trade Towers are the tallest buildings in New York.” Were I to make these statements, would my “relating to the world” make them true? Finally, it is always easy to equate the idea that we all believe our beliefs to be true with the different idea that everything each of us believes is true; that each of us deserves respect for our views with the different idea that all our views are equally well-founded, reality-oriented, etc. There are indeed only views from somewhere—many somewheres. But a view is a view precisely because it sees something that is not a view. For an eloquent explication of this idea I refer Goldberg to Truth and Method by the German philosopher Hans-Georg Gadamer. I do wish both philosophers and psychoanalysts would stop dividing up the world of philosophy into “analytic” folk and the rest, when so many philosophers from both sides of this reputed divide are engaged in dialogue with each other. In criticism of Donald Davidson, Goldberg quotes Travis: “a particular way the world might be is not structured in one intrinsic way.” I'm afraid I do not understand this sentence. As for the other passage he quotes, I don't get its critical force.

[[RunningHead]]

Individuality, The Impossible Project: Psychoanalysis and Self-Creation

Carlo Strenger. Madison, CT: International Universities Press, 1998, v + 251 pp., $35.00 hardcover, $22.00 paperback

Stuart Twemlow

Stuart Twemlow

1417 Bigelow Commons
Enfield, CT 06082-3349
Fax: 860-253-9522
E-mail: Stwemlow@aol.com

Stuart Twemlow   Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, University of Kansas School of Medicine.

This is an exciting book. Before beginning an analysis of it, I want to give you my overall personal reactions. The book woke me up in much the same way as did Bion's readings. I'm not implying that Strenger is yet a Bion, but his ideas were an awakening, and seemed to clarify several nagging questions in my mind that I'd been tussling with: for example, that psychoanalytic ideas of what is a healthy outcome for treatment are so dreadfully middle-class. They lack the hideous passion of extremism and the fascinating perverse energy of deviance. Strenger deals with these issues and others, often raising more questions than answers.

The epilogue to the book captures Strenger's position: “The contemporary psychoanalytic clinician cannot rely on unquestioned normative models of human development and maturity in working with these patients” (p. 227). Instead, Strenger raises the question, “To what extent is individuality the realization of a pre-existing nature, and to what extent is it something like an artistic creation?” (p. 227). The book sensitively layers history and tradition, the current culture, early childhood, and the influence of parents and other critical object relationships on what he calls his patients' search for authorship, the process by which they create themselves. This authoring of individuality aided by therapy Strenger calls the “highly private ritualized search for the self” (p. 229). He severely eschews quick-fix brief therapy, whose facile “cures” he analogizes to religious conversion.

Lawrence Friedman Replies

Lawrence Friedman

Lawrence Friedman

129B East 71st Street
New York, NY 10021

Lawrence Friedman  

June 22, 2002. Arnold Goldberg informs me that when I refer to an object in the world (visible in different perspectives), I am picturing myself as someone from an outside vantage point “looking out at and then representing (or characterizing) the world.” Mine is, I am told, a “view from nowhere,” a “spectator view of the world.” Enlightened by this exegesis, I now understand that when Arnold Goldberg recognizes “that we are always in the world,” he is not only an outside spectator who is looking at a world inhabited by an in-the-world Arnold Goldberg; he is also an Arnold Goldberg being spied upon by the vigilant Arnold Goldberg who sees how snugly he is stuck there. Is that, I wonder, an illustration of the famous vertical split that the inner? outer? Arnold Goldberg has written about so interestingly?

1312

With a Woman's Voice: A Writer's Struggle for Emotional Freedom.

Lucy Daniels. Latham, MD: Madison Books, 320 pp., $27.95.

Arlene Richards

Arlene Richards

200 East 89th Street
New York, NY 11102
Fax: 212-427-0585
E-mail: 73441.3427@compuserve.com

Arlene Richards  

This book records a life lived on a large stage, the examined life of a remarkable woman. Born into a family of larger-than-life achievements and terrible tragedy, Lucy Daniels has tried to make sense of what she experienced as an unhappy child, a miserable adolescent, and a troubled adult. Writing as a sixty-year-old woman, she characterizes her early childhood as a series of inchoate attempts to make her mother and father love her. As she recalls her earliest memories, she laudably refrains from attributing to herself as a child knowledge gained only later. It is astonishing that she recalls so much, especially in view of the memory loss associated with the many shock treatments she was subjected to as a teenage anorexic.

Her anorexia was life-threatening. Her first hospitalization for it was precipitated by her being sent home from boarding school, having starved herself down to sixty pounds despite doing excellent academic work, having many friends, and being a leader in school government.

[[RunningHead]]

Early Freud and Late Freud: Reading Anew Studies on Hysteria and Moses and Monotheism

Ilse Grubrich-Simitis, transl. Philip Slotkin. London: Routledge, 1997, 119 pp, $28.95.

Richard Bernstein

Richard Bernstein

66 Fifth Avenue
New York, NY 10003
Fax: 212-229-5315
E-mail: Bernster@newschool.edu

Richard Bernstein  

Ilse Grubrich-Simitis is one of the finest readers and interpreters of Freud's texts. She combines a number of virtues that are all too rare. As an editor at S. Fischer Verlag, the German publisher of Freud, she worked closely on the publication of Freud's texts. She has in addition made a thorough study of Freud's unpublished manuscripts and correspondence. A practicing psychoanalyst with an ear and an eye for the nuanced meanings of slight variations, she is also a sharp critic of the Freud-bashing that has become an international sport. In her Back to Freud's Texts (Grubrichs-Simitis 1996), which bears the subtitle Making Silent Documents Speak, she has already demonstrated how paying meticulous attention to Freud's unpublished drafts and manuscripts deepens and enriches our understanding of his texts. This is a matter not of idle pedantry, but rather of showing in detail how a subtle appreciation of context, as well as of Freud's working habits, enables us to make his texts speak in new and fresh ways.

The present book consists of an introduction and two previously published essays. In the introduction she forcefully responds to the virulent attacks on psychoanalysis that have become so popular. She is most irritated and offended by the tendency to make all sorts of outrageous claims about what Freud presumably said and did that are clearly refuted by turning to his texts. Her two essays are intended to illustrate what we can learn when we adopt the attitude of “evenly suspended attention” that the psychoanalyst adopts in respect to an analysand. The first essay deals with Studies on Hysteria, coauthored with Breuer, the second with Moses and Monotheism, written some forty years later—the last book Freud published in his lifetime. It might seem odd to bring these two essays together in a single book.

[[RunningHead]]

Healing the Soul in the Age of the Brain: Becoming Conscious in an Unconscious World

Elio Frattaroli. New York: Viking Penguin, 2001, 462 pp., $25.95

Howard Covitz

Howard Covitz

24 Latham Parkway
Melrose Park, PA 19027-3148
E-mail: Hhcovitz@aol.com

Howard Covitz   Institute for Psychoanalytic Psychotherapies, Melrose Park, PA.

With the lion's share of copies of the Dream Book still on the publisher's shelf, Freud was moved to write popular works for the lay public, notably the much lighter works on wit and parapraxes and, later, the two-part Introductory Lectures. Many since have tried to make the flavor of the psychoanalytic process accessible to the interested public.

Elio Frattaroli's work stands in this tradition but is unique in the beauty of its writing and in its cogent integration of a theory of treatment with models for the good life and for consciousness. Healing the Soul is further distinguished by its rich case material, including a demonstration of countertransference struggles; a closing section discusses the transference-like struggles that may arise in giving life to a volume such as this. Frattaroli, after presenting an original analysis of Freud's battle to transcend materialist/positivist thinking, offers a solution to Freud's dilemma by (1) tying Waelder's theory of multiple function to Bohr's theory of complementarity and (2) highlighting the distinction between Freud's early libido model and the later and more encompassing Eros model. In the clinical centerpiece of this work (the treatment of Mary), Frattaroli demonstrates how an analytic relationship comes into being as an interweaving of the processes and processing of both members of the dyad and their separate but resonating feeling states. Here he invokes Bohr's discussion of the ubiquitous presence of the scientist in the structure and conclusions of all scientific experiments. Overall, this volume is a feast for the reader; it aligns analysis with various trends in contemporary scientific thought and assumes a comfortable middle ground between one- and two-person psychologies.

Frattaroli's intention, beyond providing a scientific basis for analysis that is neither mechanistic nor materialist, is to insist on the value, for analyst and analysand alike, of embracing the conflicts of life. These are seen to arise, to use Frattaroli's terms, between the It, grounded in its biology, the I, functioning with its stereotypical patterns and repetition compulsion, and the I-that-stands-above, which imparts a transcendent and spiritual-moral function to what otherwise would be animal instinct.

Kleinians: Psychoanalysis Inside Out

Janet Sayers. Cambridge: Polity Press, 2000, 242 pp., $50.00 hardcover, $14.99 paperback.

James Gooch

James Gooch

308 South Rodeo Drive
Beverly Hills, CA 90212
Fax: 310-556-3714
E-mail: Drsgooch@aol.com

James Gooch   Training and Supervising Analyst, Psychoanalytic Center of California; Training and Supervising Analyst, Southern California Psychoanalytic Institute; Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, University of Southern California School of Medicine.

Janet Sayers is a teacher of psychoanalytic psychology in the sociology department of the University of Kent, a psychotherapist, and a writer. In about 200 pages, which include an introduction linking key elements of Freud's work and the ideas of Klein, she writes about the Kleinian revolution and evolution in a review of ten Kleinians that makes use of accounts of their personal and public lives, their families, and their patients. She does this in ten chapters, one about each person: Melanie Klein, “Discovering Inner Realities”; Susan Isaacs, “Children's Phantasies”; Joan Riviere, “Gender Masquerades”; Adrien Stokes, “Ballet and Art”; Herbert Rosenfeld, “Schizophrenics and Gangsters”; Wilfred Bion, “Group and Individual Analysis”; Esther Bick, “Infant Observation”; Francis Tustin, “Anorexia and Autism”; Hanna Segal, “Symbolism and Psychosis”; and Ronald Britton, “Exclusions and Elegies.”

From Sayers's acknowledgments at the beginning of the book of those family members and colleagues who provided biographical and other details, to the extensive bibliographies in the notes near the end, it is clear that much thorough and painstaking work went into the writing of this book. The same care shows in the index, in the photographs of each person at the start of his or her chapter, and in the development of the chapters themselves. As implied by the subtitle “Psychoanalysis Inside Out,” Sayers in her chapters describes the links between her subjects' life experiences and the evolution of their particular psychoanalytic contributions—the interplay of internal reality and external reality. She uses whatever biographical material she has been able to glean from letters, biographies, and so forth, to infer the personal experiences, traumas, struggles, and life experiences that may feasibly have contributed to the particular sensitivities of each writer. This in turn helps us to understand how they came to focus on what they did in their writings, the particular interactions in them of outer and inner reality, of love and hate, from infancy onwards in health and illness and maladaptation.

[[RunningHead]]

Freud, Leonardo Da Vinci, and The Vulture's Tail: A Refreshing Look At Leonardo's Sexuality.

Wayne Andersen New York: Other Press, 2001, 256 pp., $48.00 hardcover, $22.00 paperback

Janice Coco

Janice Coco

8510 SW 41st Avenue
Portland, OR 97219
E-mail: Jmcoco@earthlink.net

Janice Coco  

Every so many years, new scholarship emerges to reawaken the controversy engendered by Freud's 1910 analysis of Leonardo da Vinci. Wayne Andersen's Freud, Leonardo da Vinci, and the Vulture's Tail revisits this intellectual fray with a remarkable intensity, as the author works through the various competing theories, including that of Freud. Andersen attempts to untangle the “mare's nest” that has been made of Leonardo's words and sexual identity (pp. 4, 24).

Readers who over the years have followed the trajectory of criticism elicited by Freud's infamous error will perhaps be put off by the rehashing of events implied by Andersen's title. Nevertheless, the author extends the already complex literature, offering an alternative explanation of the artist's sexuality and reexamining Freud's counter-transference in relation to Leonardo. For this reason, it is a worthy book for those interested in the practice and origins of applied psychoanalysis in the arts. Inasmuch as the validity of psychobiography has been challenged by art historians for ignoring crucial art historical evidence and, in turn, by psychoanalysts for the frequent lack of a living (or cooperative) analysand, it seems that the interdisciplinarian risks incurring the antagonism of both disciplines. In the specific instance of Freud's Leonardo, most authors have favored one discipline at the expense of the other; consequently, most of the ongoing criticism has been aimed not so much at conclusions as at methodology. Theoretically, it appears that psychoanalysis and art history are frequently at odds with each other, even though the creative process inherent to the practices of artmaking and therapy is potentially the same. As an art historian versed in psychoanalytic theory, Andersen casts a critical eye in both directions.

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

From Monologue to Dialogue

A Transition in Psychoanalytic Practice

Theodore Shapiro

Theodore Shapiro

525 East 68th Street
New York, NY 10021
Fax: 212-746-5944
E-mail: Tshapiro@med.cornell.edu

Theodore Shapiro  

During the latter part of the twentieth century, psychoanalysts of various stripes espoused the move from free association and neutrality to various forms of intersubjectivity and dialogue. This shift is studied from the vantage point of conversational rules in terms of the shift from monologue to dialogue, using the concepts of semantics, syntax, and pragmatics. Viewing the data of analysis in this manner offers a means of evaluating the contributions of both monologue and dialogue to our understanding of the conduct of an analysis and the kind of information that can be expected to emerge. Exclusive devotion to either stance, it is argued, renders less understanding than would emerge from a balanced use of both.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Mourning Analysis

The Post-Termination Phase

Heather Craige

Heather Craige

3404 Churchill Road
Raleigh, NC 27607
Fax: 919-787-0432
E-mail: Hcraige@ipass.net

Heather Craige  

One hundred twenty-one analytic candidates who had completed training analysis responded to a survey about their post-termination experience. Seventy-six percent of respondents experienced a mourning process that lasted on average between six months and a year, while 24 per cent experienced no discernible sense of painful loss. Twenty candidates were interviewed to obtain a deeper understanding of the mourning process that follows analysis. During the post-termination phase, the analysand's self-analytic capacity is tested in the struggle to contain and understand feelings about the loss of the analyst, as well as transference reactions triggered by that loss. After a “good-enough analysis,” the analysand internalizes not only the analyst's functions and attitudes toward him or her, but also a sustaining, positive internal image of the analyst. Four cases illustrate unexpected difficulties that may emerge during the post-termination phase when the loss of the analyst is experienced as a repetition of earlier, traumatic losses or as a rupture of an unanalyzed, selfobject transference.

responseSet.title: The Challenges of Psychoanalytic Developmental Theory
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Challenges of Psychoanalytic Developmental Theory

Phyllis Tyson

Phyllis Tyson

677 Turquoise Street.
La Jolla, CA 92037-1807
Fax: 619-452-1467
E-mail: Phyllis@tysonz.com

Phyllis Tyson  

As we look back over the history of psychoanalysis and ahead to the future, we might agree that no theoretical area has seen more controversy, or presented more challenges, than psychoanalytic developmental theory. The challenge of understanding the mind and feelings of a child and the ways this understanding might provide a lens through which to view the mind of the adult has led psychoanalysts to propose numerous theories of conscious and unconscious mental functioning. As experience and reflection reveal the explicatory failures and other shortcomings of these theories, they are modified or replaced and the cycle starts again.

What follows is a commentary on the evolution of psychoanalytic developmental theory, interwoven with the whole of psychoanalytic theory, and on the challenges it currently faces—and poses. This will not be an historical overview or critical exegesis. Instead, I have chosen to use the vicissitudes of the theories of affect and affect regulation as a way of approaching the broader issues. I have chosen this strategy because of the centrality of feeling states to psychic life, interpersonal interaction, the clinical situation, and the developmental process, and because competence in affect regulation influences the balance between impulse and action, strengthens the capacity to tolerate frustration, ambiguity, and ambivalence, and contributes to complexity in object relations and social interaction. I have chosen it also because of a contemporary clinical urgency to find effective means of prevention and treatment for children unable to regulate affect, and because the quest to understand the nature of affect has led to the evolution of several models of the mind. Using very broad strokes, I will try to paint a picture of past and current psychoanalytic views of affect (for a comprehensive summary of affect theory, see Stein 1991)

Arnold Goldberg  

The author considers the concept of enactment as a ubiquitous event that is best seen as part of a sequence in the process of understanding a patient. As such, enactments are not unusual or special save as they are often subject to disavowal or to being singled out by the analyst as especially subject to scrutiny. Once recognized, enactments need to be interpreted: not so much in terms of their unconscious origins, but more with regard to the need to include them in the analytic dialogue.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Psychoanalytic Hypothesis Concerning the Therapeutic Action of SSRI Medications

Richard Gottlieb

Richard Gottlieb

263 West End Avenue
Apartment 1-E
New York, NY 10023
E-mail: Rmgottlieb@aol.com

Richard Gottlieb  

Psychoanalysis yet to fulfill the promise that its theory and unique form of listening would contribute to our understanding of some effects of psychotropic medications. Analysts are said to listen to their patients as do no other students of mind/brain, yet we have offered no interpretation of the therapeutic benefits of highly effective SSRI (selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor) medications.

One confounding factor has been that the SSRIs challenge traditional diagnostic schemes. They bring about improvement in disorders that were not previously thought to be related to each other, such as depression, obsessive-compulsive disorder, and social anxiety. The successes of medications across diagnostic categories suggest unrecognized commonalities among them. Taken together with certain readily observed clinical phenomena (see below), these facts suggest that the essential benefits of these medications derive from their capacity to modify aggression.

Few clinicians can have failed to note the repeated reports of successfully medicated depressives to the effect that “I am no longer angry in the way I used to be.” The details vary, but these reports are consistent around the theme of the patient's being less inclined toward rages, less irritable. Events that in the past might have provoked fury are now merely irritating: “They roll off me.” Some people do not spontaneously volunteer these reports, but they will speak of the change if asked. From others one might only hear, “My friends say I'm easier to get along with.” And most evident of all is a less hurtful attitude toward the self.

Faculty, New York Psychoanalytic Institute; Associate Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Albert Einstein College of Medicine.

Submitted for publication May 23, 2002.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Priority of Primary Process Categorizing

Experimental Evidence Supporting a Psychoanalytic Developmental Hypothesis

Linda Brakel , Howard Shevrin and Karen Villa

Karen Villa

525 Third Street
Ann Arbor, MI 48103
Fax: 734-764-3506
E-Mail: Brakel@umich.edu

Karen Villa  

Earlier work has provided experimental evidence for the existence of the primary and secondary process mental organization posited by Freud and has demonstrated that primary process effects are the more active unconsciously (Brakel et al. 2000). Primary and secondary processes were assessed by a categorization test in which qualitatively different principles could be used. In new experiments using the same stimuli, another significant implication of Freud's model was tested: that primary process mental organization has developmental priority. In these experiments, which studied 559 participants ranging in age from 3 to 80, it was found (1) that primary process mentation predominates in preschoolers; (2) that it is not until around age 7 that primary process organization is supplanted by secondary process organization; and (3) that after age 7 the predominance of secondary process organization remains remarkably stable throughout the life span.

[[RunningHead]]

A New Language for Psychoanalytic Diagnosis

Jonathan Shedler

Jonathan Shedler

2450 South Vine Street
Denver, CO 80208
E-mail: Jonathan@shedler.com

Jonathan Shedler  

Many psychoanalysts believe it is impossible to conduct empirical research without eviscerating or trivializing psychoanalytic constructs, and past research efforts have all too often reinforced this view. A new method for studying personality and personality pathology is presented that challenges such beliefs. This method, the Shedler-Westen Assessment Procedure (SWAP), captures the richness and complexity of psychoanalytic constructs and formulations while also providing reliable data for research. The method is being used to develop a new personality disorder taxonomy, as an alternative to the DSM, that is both empirically grounded and psychoanalytically relevant. Its role in psychoanalytic training and supervision is discussed, as is its value as a measure of structural change in psychoanalytic process and outcome research.

responseSet.title: Developments in Cognitive Neuroscience: I. Conflict, Compromise, and Connectionism
responseSet.authorMast: Drew Westen and  Glen O. Gabbard
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Developments in Cognitive Neuroscience

I. Conflict, Compromise, and Connectionism

Drew Westen and Glen Gabbard

Glen Gabbard

648 Beacon Street, 6th floor
Boston, MA 02215
E-mail: Dwesten@bu.edu

One Baylor Plaza
Houston, TX 77030
Fax: 713-798-3138
E-mail: Ggabbard12@aol.com

Glen Gabbard  

The strength of psychoanalysis has always been its understanding of affect and motivation. Contemporary developments in cognitive neuro-science offer possibilities of integrating sophisticated, experimentally informed models of thought and memory with an understanding of dynamically and clinically meaningful processes. Aspects of contemporary theory and research in cognitive neuroscience are integrated with psychoanalytic theory and technique, particularly theories of conflict and compromise. After a description of evolving models of the mind in cognitive neuroscience, several issues relevant to psychoanalytic theory and practice are addressed. These include the nature of representations, the interaction of cognition and affect, and the mechanisms by which the mind unconsciously forges compromise solutions that best fit multiple cognitive and affective-motivational constraints.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Guards at the Gate

Race, Resistance, and Psychic Reality

Forrest Hamer

Forrest Hamer

5305 College Avenue
Oakland, CA 94618
Fax: 510-654-2177
E-mail: fhamer8580@aol.com

Forrest Hamer  

Racial difference and similarity in the treatment relationship highlight the question of what is real and apparently psychological in the different racial experiences of patients and therapists. The relationship between racial material and resistance is discussed, as well as the constancy and fluidity of race as intrapsychic content and the distinction psychoanalysts draw between social and psychic reality. Race in the mind, it is argued, is determined by both internal and external factors, and both are important in the analysis of racial material in the transference.
