responseSet.title: Developments in Cognitive Neuroscience: II. Implications for Theories of Transference
responseSet.authorMast: Drew Westen and  Glen O. Gabbard
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Developments in Cognitive Neuroscience

II. Implications for Theories of Transference

Drew Westen and Glen Gabbard

Glen Gabbard

648 Beacon Street, 6th floor
Boston, MA 02215
E-mail: Dwesten@bu.edu

One Baylor Plaza
Houston, TX 77030
Fax: 713-798-3138
E-mail: Ggabbard12@aol.com

Glen Gabbard  

An integration of psychoanalytic theory with contemporary developments in cognitive neuroscience offers a useful perspective on long-standing controversies about the nature of transference, and a better understanding of the precise mechanisms by which transferential processes occur. Contemporary psychoanalytic views of transference are reviewed, and the many processes that constitute transference are described. Two issues that have emerged in different guises for several decades—the role of the analyst in eliciting transference, and the nature of “real” and “transferential” components of the therapeutic relationship—are reconsidered in the light of concepts such as connectionist networks. Although a useful analytic stance is one that allows the patient's enduring dynamics to dominate the analytic field, it is suggested, anonymity is neither a cognitive possibility nor the driving force behind most transference reactions, and the distinction between “real” and “transferential” perceptions is one of therapeutic interest, not of mechanism. Certain features of the analytic situation make some dynamics more likely than others to enter the treatment relationship, notably those related to authority, intimacy and attachment, and sexuality. Transference reactions are best understood as constructed from a combination of the patient's enduring dispositions to react in particular ways under particular conditions; features of the analytic situation and of the analyst; and interactions between patient and analyst. These reactions do not unfold ineluctably from the patient's mind in the consulting room, nor are they cognitive constructions of the patient-analyst dyad or co-constructions of relatively equal partners exerting their influence on the analytic field.

[[RunningHead]]

Tennessee Williams

The Uses of Declarative Memory in the Glass Menagerie

Daniel Jacobs

Daniel Jacobs

64 Williston Road
Brookline, MA 02146
E-mail: Danjacobs@rcn.com

Daniel Jacobs  

Tennessee Williams called his first great work, The Glass Menagerie, his “memory play.” The situation in which Williams found himself when he began writing the play is explored, as are the ways in which he used the declarative memory of his protagonist, Tom Wingfield, to express and deal with his own painful conflicts. Williams's use of stage directions, lighting, and music to evoke memory and render it three-dimensional is described. Through a close study of The Glass Menagerie, the many uses of memory for the purposes of wish fulfillment, conflict resolution, and resilience are examined

[[RunningHead]]

Rethinking Homosexuality

What it Teaches us about Psychoanalysis

Ralph Roughton

Ralph Roughton

240 Halah Circle
Atlanta, GA 30328
E-mail: Ralphroughton@aol.com

Ralph Roughton  

This presentation, written ten years after the American Psychoanalytic Association adopted a nondiscrimination policy with regard to sexual orientation, discusses the evolving relationship between psychoanalysis and homosexuality. The paper is in three sections: (1) the process of policy change and the overturning of injustice; (2) the excursion after Freud's death into analytic bias and extreme pathologizing of homosexuality, the struggle to overcome those distortions, and the lessons to be learned from this history; and (3) the search, in the coming decade, for new understanding of homosexuality and all aspects of sexuality.

[[RunningHead]]

On Reality and Objectivity

Marcia Cavell

Marcia Cavell

1570 Olympus Avenue
Berkeley, CA 94708
E-mail: Cavell@uclink4.berkeley.edu

Marcia Cavell  

July 23, 1999. As a trained philosopher, and a clinician in training, I am often frustrated and dismayed by the casual swing some of my clinical betters take at complex philosophical questions. So I was delighted that both Lawrence Friedman J. Amer. Psychoanal. Assn.47/2, 401) and Charles Hanly (J. Amer. Psychoanal. Assn.47/2, 427) spoke up with such sophistication on the side I have recently been arguing (Cavell 1993, 1998a, b). Hanly quite rightly says that epistemological subjectivism is self-defeating. (He explains in a particularly lucid way, incidentally, some prevalent confusions in the understanding of quantum mechanics.) Friedman insists that we are justified in making claims about how things “really” are.

More in the spirit of friendly amendment than criticism, I want to take up a few points in Friedman's essay that may undermine his own case for “reality.”

In tracing the historical move toward a postmodern kind of relativism, Friedman (p. 410) describes Quine as holding “that our reasoning is supported not by logic but by the success of our species; even the strictest reasoning, he insists, is more a matter of belief than of justification.” Friedman's reference is to an essay by Quine called “Five Milestones of Empiricism” (1981). The particular passage I believe Friedman has in mind reads as follows: “The fifth [milestone] brings naturalism: abandonment of the goal of first philosophy. It sees natural science as an inquiry into reality, fallible and corrigible but not answerable to any supra-scientific tribunal, and not in need of any justification beyond observation and the hypothetico-deductive method …” (p. 72).

319

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis and Schizophrenia

Three Responses to Martin Willick

Elio Frattaroli

Elio Frattaroli

One Bala Avenue, Suite 210
Bala Cynwyd, PA 19004-3207
E-mail: Elio@home.com

Elio Frattaroli  

May 9, 2001. Martin Willick's “Psychoanalysis and Schizophrenia: A Cautionary Tale” (J. Amer. Psychoanal. Assn.49/1) typifies what I consider a very dangerous trend in current psychoanalytic thinking. Willick rejects all psychoanalytic theories of schizophrenia and all psychoanalytic evidence about the etiology of schizophrenia because, he claims, “The neurobiological research of the past twenty-five years offers compelling evidence that the etiology of schizophrenia is, to put it succinctly, biological rather than psychogenic” (p. 29). He then suggests that the same may prove to be true for borderline personality disorder as well. I disagree. There is no scientific or psychoanalytic evidence whatsoever to support this kind of either/or dichotomizing—considering some symptoms psychogenic and others biological.

I first became aware that analysts had begun to think this way approximately ten years ago, when I attended a discussion group on “analytic process in patients treated with antidepressant medication” at a Fall Meeting of the American. I was astonished at how many colleagues there—young and old alike—were accepting without question that major depression is a brain disease having nothing to do with inner conflict and impervious to psychoanalytic treatment. In fact, out of fifty or so analysts in the group, I was the only one who clearly and coherently objected to this view. I argued that even the most profoundly “biological” depression must necessarily happen within a psychological context and must be understood, like any other symptom, in terms of inner conflict. Before that meeting I had assumed that all psychoanalysts shared this view. Since then I have come to realize that our psychoanalytic understanding of human nature is being severely eroded by the materialistic prejudice of biological psychiatry. As Willick's article vividly illustrates, we are so impressed and/or intimidated by the claims of Big Science that we are ready to abandon the hard-won insights of clinical psychoanalysis and defer to the reductionistic, pseudoscientific belief (an improvable philosophical assumption) that symptoms are merely byproducts of bad brain chemistry. We no longer believe that inner conflict is the central unifying explanation for all psychiatric symptoms.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis

Practice and Technique

Lawrence Friedman

Lawrence Friedman

129B East 71st Street
New York, NY 10021
Fax: 212-734-3399

Lawrence Friedman  

I suspect that international readers will find this a very North American issue of JAPA. Despite the enormous influence of Klein and Bion on the West Coast, the many reviews here of Kleinian books show mostly an appreciative, wide-eyed fascination—a stranger's hospitality—rather than a family entanglement (see Margulies's review of Mitrani). An issue on another topic—epistemology, theory of the mind, therapeutic action—might have had a more cosmopolitan flavor, and tapped a more worldwide concern.

Have practice and technique, then, become especially problematic for North Americans? Such a generalization is hardly warranted. Analysts everywhere contribute to all our discussions. But few would deny that North Americans as a whole are peculiarly unsettled on the question of the analyst's place. American analysts often seem as puzzled by their own role as they are by their patients' transferences. Colleagues abroad wonder why Americans can't just take the analytic set-up for granted and get on with the work. As though they were building analysis all over again, Americans wonder about neutrality … enactment … goals and who gets to set them … negotiations between analyst and patient … compliance, etc.—the topics that predominate in this issue of JAPA.

Why are so many North Americans obsessed in this way? Of course, the “widening scope” challenges settled customs (see Waugaman's review of Giovacchini), but this cannot be the root of American concern, for North Americans are not foremost in the theorizing about difficult patients, and, in any case, illness shows no evident demography.

Much of the answer is surely the inspiration and challenge offered by home-grown interpersonal and relational schools and their offshoots, as amply illustrated in the following pages (see Slochower's review of Frankel). And the lack of a deeply rooted intellectualistic tradition makes Americans more skeptical about theorizing, less proud of analytic interpretations, less enamored of language. Shapiro (2002)

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Analyst's Uncertainty and Fear

Leslie Jordan

Leslie Jordan

3955 East Exposition Avenue
Suite 405
Denver, CO 80209
E-mail: Lesliej@compuserve.com

Leslie Jordan  

Jill Miller opened the panel by noting that openness to uncertainty is central to analytic work; without that, we lose touch with our patients. Yet uncertainty may bring with it anxiety and fear. Our role is to tolerate and metabolize affective states, but at times when fear overwhelms the ego, to do this may seem impossible. The biologically driven response to fear is to fight or to flee, options that we do not allow ourselves in the consulting room. If these urges press toward imminent action, we experience what Racker calls “drowning in the countertransference.” Miller introduced the panelists and invited them to discuss experiences of uncertainty and fear in the clinical situation.

Warren Poland was the first presenter. He reminded us that analysis is not a theatre from which we can go home safely. It “is not a spectator sport, and risk-feeling emotional danger-comes with the analytic territory.”

Poland gave examples of how closeness can make us part of a patient's frightening internal world. When a paranoid patient unexpectedly erupted in rage against him, he experienced sudden disorientation and fright, feeling as if he were in a childhood nightmare. With another patient, he felt a buildup of dread as, session after session, he faced her “militant” hopelessness. Anticipating her inevitable suicide, he feared the destruction of his reputation, his financial security, and even his family's well-being.

Dangers also arise out of the perils of reality, Poland pointed out. “Patients remind us, often when we are least prepared, of just those parts of life about which we least want to think.

Panel held at the Spring Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, New Orleans, May 5, 2001. Panelists: Jill Miller (chair), Judith Fingert Chused, Steven A. Frankel, and Warren S. Poland.

989

responseSet.title: The Problem of Self-Disclosure in Psychoanalysis
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Problem of Self-Disclosure in Psychoanalysis

W. Meissner

W. Meissner

Chestnut Hill, MA 02467
Fax: 617-522-2380
E-mail: Meissner@bc.edu

W. Meissner  

The problem of self-disclosure is explored in relation to currently shifting paradigms of the nature of the analytic relation and analytic interaction. Relational and intersubjective perspectives emphasize the role of self-disclosure as not merely allowable, but as an essential facilitating aspect of the analytic dialogue, in keeping with the role of the analyst as a contributing partner in the process. At the opposite extreme, advocates of classical anonymity stress the importance of neutrality and abstinence. The paper seeks to chart a course between unconstrained self-disclosure and absolute anonymity, both of which foster misalliances. Self-disclosure is seen as at times contributory to the analytic process, and at times deleterious. The decision whether to self-disclose, what to disclose, and when and how, should be guided by the analyst's perspective on neutrality, conceived as a mental stance in which the analyst assesses and decides what, at any given point, seems to contribute to the analytic process and the patient's therapeutic benefit. The major risk in self-disclosure is the tendency to draw the analytic interaction into the real relation between analyst and patient, thus diminishing or distorting the therapeutic alliance, mitigating transference expression, and compromising therapeutic effectiveness.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Sexuality and Textuality

Bonnie Litowitz

Bonnie Litowitz

180 North Michigan Avenue
Suite 2220
Chicago, IL 60601
E-mail: Belitowitz@aol.com

Bonnie Litowitz  

Post-Freudian theories have been criticized for abandoning what is basic to psychoanalysis: the biological body and sexuality as the source of intrapsychic motivation. Arguably, however, they are more present than ever before—for example, in explanations by theorists who propose therapeutic actions beyond interpretation, presymbolic enactments of procedural memories, or disclosures of the analyst's bodily states as an aspect of intersubjectivity. By contrast, the Freudian body was always a text whose mediated meanings require interpretation, for which Freud provided eloquent guides. It is this textuality, and not sexuality, that distinguishes a psychoanalytic approach: a psycho-logic constructed according to a grammar of desire that mediates experience and creates interpretable behavior, both in action and in speech. Theoretical changes in psychoanalysis are traced historically along the dimension of textuality, the example of perversion is invoked, and the conclusion drawn that any theoretical approach, traditional or post-Freudian, that expands an understanding of textuality contributes to the science of psychoanalysis.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Transference as Defense

Jacob Arlow

Jacob Arlow

94 Wildwood Road
Kingspoint, NY 11024

Jacob Arlow  

I had intended to title this paper “The Dynamics of Transference,” until I realized that this was precisely the title Freud used in introducing the subject. Perhaps no subject in psychoanalysis has been as thoroughly explored and examined as the subject of transference. For Freud, transference was one of the defining qualities of psychoanalysis. Today analysis of the transference in the therapeutic situation remains the central technical imperative, as enunciated by Freud. He made this clear on many occasions. “Finally,” he said, “every conflict has to be fought out in the sphere of transference” (Freud 1912a, p. 104). He advised beginning analysts not to worry about being able to make the correct interpretation of material. That happens automatically and with little effort. The important thing is to keep one's eye on the transference (1912b). One does not communicate any interpretation,” he wrote, “until a dependable transference has developed.… the first stage of treating consists of attaching [the patient] to the treatment and to the person of the physician. To ensure this, one need do nothing but allow him time.” In characteristic fashion, Freud summed up his attitude on the subject in an epigram: “For when all is said and done, it is impossible to destroy anyone in absentia or in effigy” (Freud 1912a, p. 108).

Although much has changed in psychoanalysis over the years, the basic influence of these ideas has persisted. Our teaching about transference remains fundamentally unchanged as regards the course of the psychoanalytic process. Characteristically, analytic treatment is divided into three phases. In the first, or opening, phase, the analyst learns about the patient's difficulty. In the second phase, the analyst is instructed tor the most part to do whatever possible to strengthen the transference attachment. Then, “when … the treatment has obtained mastery over the patient, … the whole of his illness's new production is concentrated upon a single point—his relation to the doctor.

[[RunningHead]]

The Triadic Match

The Interactive Effect of Supervisor, Candidate, and Patient

Judy Kantrowitz

Judy Kantrowitz

334 Kent Street
Brookline, MA 02446
Fax: 617-277-9564
E-mail: Judy_kantrowitz@hms.harvard.edu

Judy Kantrowitz  

The triadic match is the author's term for the interaction among analytic candidate, supervisor, and patient. Overlapping or diverging characteristics of candidate and supervisor may influence the candidate's learning for good or ill depending on the way patient's and candidate's character and conflicts interact. Four candidates who had found their supervisors' character and supervisory styles particularly beneficial in relation to a particular patient volunteered to describe their experiences. Candidates and supervisors were interviewed. The aim of the paper is to illuminate factors in the match that enhanced or interfered with the candidates' learning. While the paper is presented from the perspective of the candidates' experiences, the balance between challenge and comfort in learning situations is also considered.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Slow Magic

Psychoanalysis and “The Disenchantment of the World”

Joel Whitebook

Joel Whitebook

1225 Park Avenue, Suite IA
New York, NY 10128
Fax. 212-585-4974
E-mail: Whitebookj@aol.com

Joel Whitebook  

Freud's work can be situated in terms of the debate between Enlightenment and anti-Enlightenment thought. The attempts of both sides to claim Freud for their position have merit, but they miss the crucial point: namely, that the tension between its Enlightenment and anti-Enlightenment tendencies is what gives Freud's thinking much of its vitality and depth. The task that faces the interpreter is therefore to elucidate that tension and assess the alignment of forces between the two strands in his thought. An examination of the concept of magic in Freud's theory provides an opportunity to pursue this interpretive task. Although the Enlightenment position he often seems to embrace advocates the complete elimination of magic, many “magical” elements remain in his theory and clinical practice. Nor should this situation be deplored, for the ambition to completely exorcise “enchantment” from human experience is one of the misguided excesses of the Enlightenment. The question of an appropriate fate for magic in psychoanalysis is discussed in relation to the vicissitudes of the transference. Finally, the science versus hermenueutics debate is examined in the light of these considerations in an attempt to specify the unique nature of the psychoanalytic experience

This work, in which André Green consolidates the groundbreaking reorientation of psychoanalytic thought he began some twenty years before the present volume, is important and stimulating. The author here places his ideas under the rubric of the “negative,” a topic he divides into normal and pathological. The normal work of the negative shows itself as the indirection instituted by repression and other defenses to protect the mental apparatus from becoming overwhelmed. Through such indirection individuals spare themselves the psychic truths that we analysts help our patients retrace to their unconscious roots. The work of the pathologically negative is destructive, the withdrawal of investment in the object world.

Green's originality lies in a radical reconceptualization of the metapsychological field of psychoanalysis. Because conventional wisdom has it that the model of neurosis lies at the heart of all (rather than most of)

Lawrence Friedman Responds

Lawrence Friedman

Lawrence Friedman

129B East 71st Street
New York, NY 10021
Fax: 212-734-3399

Lawrence Friedman  

September 19, 1999. I appreciate Marcia Cavell's letter as an appraisal by a psychoanalyst who is a professional philosopher, and I am pleased to find that on the philosophical issues we are at one. She does, however, gently reproach me on four counts: (1) She charges unfairness to Quine. (2) She thinks my use of the term representation is hazardous. (3) She corrects a reference to an argument of hers (Cavell 1998). And (4), to my overall thesis that the reality story should not be told in the usual way, she responds that indeed it should be.

Cavell thinks my reference to Quine is misleading. She takes Quine's lesson to be that meaning depends on context, and that knowledge is always fallible but nevertheless possible. She implies that these seemingly solid principles do not warrant my raised-eyebrow characterization. But I think it is Cavell's summary that is misleading. Quine is an interesting philosopher just because he carried those seemingly modest principles to a logical conclusion that is far more radically skeptical than others realize who maintain those principles in the general way Cavell has stated them. Quine is one of the modern thinkers who taught that every part of language and theory depends on the other parts for meaning. These thinkers challenge the notion that we can tie individual assertions and descriptions uniquely to individual facts, or establish common divisions and constancies in the world that run through different languages and theories (the famous “nontranslatability of languages” doctrine). I referred to that argument (as presented by Wittgenstein) in discussing the challenge to logical positivism. What I cited Quine for was his provocative doctrine that science teaches that nature has caused us to believe in science, although we cannot justify the belief in science. (He substituted psychology for epistemology.) Repeatedly dismissing the anticipated charge of circularity, he relied on natural science (and the argument of evolutionary adaptedness) to explain the constancies that his logic refused: How explain the constant belief in the regularity of nature? The regularities of nature have conditioned us by the regularity of reflex to believe in the regularities of nature. “… I shall not be impressed,” Quine (1969) wrote, “by protests that I am using inductive generalizations, Darwin's and others, to justify induction, and thus reasoning in a circle. The reason I shall not be impressed by this is that my position is a naturalistic one; I see philosophy not as an a priori propaedeutic or groundwork for science, but as continuous with science” (pp. 126-127).

Wallerstein on Lay Analysis and the Lawsuit

Robert Wallerstein

Robert Wallerstein

290 Beach Road
Belvedere, CA 94920
Fax: 415-789-9530

Robert Wallerstein  

May 9, 2001. As someone who has long been involved in the organizational life of the American Psychoanalytic Association, I wish to applaud Marvin Margolis's forthright exposition (J. Amer. Psychoanal. Assn.49:1) of the past decade's transformation of the American's functioning toward increasing democratization, inclusiveness, transparency, outreach to the mental health professional world and the wider concerned community, and involvement in the political process and public policy.

However, I also want to point to some errors in portraying the long, difficult, and often contentious relationship around the issue of so-called “lay analysis,” between the American and the International Psychoanalytical Association (IPA), of which the American has been from its start a component organization. This is a history about which too many, even among of the leaders of the American, have traditionally been ill informed. Margolis speaks to this history and to its culmination in the famous lawsuit against the American, in the course of which description several errors are made.

The IPA did not grant the American the exclusive right to establish new IPA groups in the United States in 1938, as Margolis stated it did (p. 13). Over the 1920s and 1930s, there had been an ongoing struggle between the Americans, who wanted to restrict psychoanalytic training to physicians (psychiatrists), and the Europeans (then the dominant majority in the IPA), who wished, following Freud's position, to keep training open to all suitable individuals, from whatever educational background.

The Americans came to the 1938 IPA Congress in Paris demanding that they be accorded the right to bar nonphysicians from psychoanalytic training in America (and to bar European-trained “lay analysts” from membership in the American should they immigrate to America), with the threat of secession from the IPA if their demand was not met.

[[RunningHead]]

Current Perspectives on the Goals of Psychoanalysis

Alice Bartlett

Alice Bartlett

The Carriage House on Holliday Park
121312 SW Western Avenue
Topeka, KS 66604
Fax: 785-234-9458
E-mail: Abartlettl@mindspring.com

Alice Bartlett  

Glen Gabbard began by highlighting Freud original struggle with the dialectic between psychoanalysis as a way to help a patient recover from an illness and psychoanalysis as a research tool for investigating the human mind. Gabbard defined a goal as an end result the analytic treatment strives to achieve. He noted that analysts, historically, have tended to eschew issues of effectiveness and outcome, a tendency that has marginalized psychoanalysis within the larger mental health community. Yet at the same time, psychoanalysis differentiates itself from other psychotherapies by positing the most ambitious goal—the alteration of fundamental personality organization. Gabbard emphasized that in this era of theoretical pluralism, our goals are as diverse as our analytic theories. In addition, analysts are never free of their unconscious agendas. Likewise, the patient brings both conscious and unconscious goals to the process, some of which may coincide or be at odds with those of the analyst. Finally, goals can change throughout the process. When are those changes evasions, and when are they constructive revisions? Gabbard then focused the panel by positing five questions for their response and discussion, followed by a summary discussion by Robert Wallerstein.

First, Gabbard asked the panelists to clarify what they mean when they speak of goals. Is there a legitimate distinction between analytic and therapeutic goals? How would they approach the distinction between process and outcome goals?

Panel held at the December Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, New York City, December 18, 1999.

Panelists: Glen O. Gabbard (chair), Arnold M. Cooper, Alain Gibeault, Owen Renik, Doris K. Silverman. Discussants: Robert S. Wallerstein in the morning, joined by Alan Skolnikoff and Sydney Pulver in the afternoon.

To determine the demographics, DSM-III-R disorders diagnosed, indications used in recommending psychoanalysis, previous treatment histories, use of medication, and length of treatment in patients in psychoanalysis in the U.S., Canada, and Australia, a mail survey of practice was sent to every other active member of the American Psychoanalytic Association and every member of the Australian Psychoanalytical Society. This supplemented an earlier survey sent to all Ontario psychoanalysts. The response rates were 40.1 % (n = 342) for the U.S., 67.2% (n = 117) for Canada, and 73.9% (n = 51) for Australia. Respondents supplied data on 1,718 patients. The employment rate for patients increases as analysis progresses (p < .0001). The mean number of concurrent categories of disorders (Axis I,3.

Norman Doidge, Training and Supervising Analyst, Canadian Institute of Psychoanalysis, Toronto Branch; research faculty, Columbia University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research; Head, Long-term Psychotherapy, Psychotherapy Program, and Assistant Professor, Department of Psychiatry, University of Toronto. Barry Simon, Assistant Professor, Department of Psychiatry, University of Toronto. Lee Brauer, consultant to the Committee on Psychoanalytic Practice, American Psychoanalytic Association. Donald C. Grant, member, Australian Psychoanalytical Society. Michael First, Research Psychiatrist, Biometrics Research Department, New York State Psychiatric Institute; Associate Professor of Clinical Psychiatry, Columbia University. Jacqueline Brunshaw, candidate, Clinical Psychology Program, York University, Toronto. William J. Lancee, Head of Research, Department of Psychiatry, Mount Sinai Hospital; Assistant Professor, Department of Psychiatry, University of Toronto. Annette Stevens, clinical faculty, Division of Community Mental Health, Department of Psychiatry, University of British Columbia. John M. Oldham, Professor and Chair, Department of Psychiatry and Behavioral Sciences, Medical University of South Carolina. Paul Mosher, founding board member, Psychoanalytic Electronic Publishing.

This work was supported by research grants from the American Psychoanalytic Association, the Australian Psychoanalytical Society, and the Toronto Psychoanalytic Society. Henry Bachrach, Norman Clemens, Charles Hanly, J. Christopher Perry, Robert Pynoos, and Robert Spitzer served as consultants. Arthur Fish, S.J., supervised ethics. Submitted for publication February 8, 2001.

575

Axis II, and Disorders First Evident in Childhood) per patient at the start of treatment is 5.01 (SD=3.66; median=4; mode=3). There are no statistically significant differences across countries. Mood, anxiety. sexual dysfunction, and personality disorders are most common. American Psychiatric Association / American Psychoanalytic Association peer review criteria for indicating psychoanalysis are followed for 86.5% of patients. Over 80% of patients in all three countries had undergone previous treatments prior to analysis. In the U.S., 18.2% of analysands are on concurrent psychoactive medication; in Australia, 9.6%. The mean length of analyses conducted in the U.S. is 5.7 years, in Australia 6.6, and in Canada 4.8. Psychoanalytic patients in all three countries have similar rates of DSM-III-R psychopathology, and many indications of chronicity.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Freud's Writing

His (W)rite of Passage and its Reverberations

Patrick Mahony

Patrick Mahony

1297 Saint Viateur
H2V IZ2 Canada
Fax: 514-276-3820
E-mail: Mahony@videotron.ca

Patrick Mahony  

The study of Freud's personal conception of writing and his use of it illuminates a significant aspect of his relationship with others, and also his sense of his own heroic greatness. In two related periods of his life, acts of writing—which were notably overdetermined in Freud and which informed as well as facilitated a distinctive kind of self-expression—gave rise to veritable (w)rites of passage. In those periods, the transferential dynamics in Freud's relationships with Fliess, his daughter Anna, and certain important analysands were expressed in ways connected with the content and process of various types of writing, including editing and translating. In sum, there is something still to be discovered in Freud's attitudes to his writing and to related intergenerational transactions between analysts and analysands who engage together in compositional activities.

[[RunningHead]]

The Hartmann Era

Martin Bergmann

Martin Bergmann

1136 Fifth Avenue
New York, NY 10128
Fax: 212-427-4947

Martin Bergmann  

June 1, 2002. I appreciate very much that Charles Brenner, at this stage in his life, took time out to review the proceedings of the symposium on the Hartmann era that I chaired (J. Amer. Psychoanal. Assn.50/1). Because important issues for the history of psychoanalysis are at stake, I wish to reply to his review. It happens we are both celebrating our ninetieth birthday this year, and since psychoanalysts, like Socrates, are both human and mortal, there are only a few of us who lived through this era and so can debate it in terms of personal experience. Four points were the subject of this symposium, which some of our most distinguished colleagues—Jacob A. Arlow, Harold Blum, André Green, William I. Grossman, Otto Kernberg, Anton Kris, Peter Neubauer, Albert Solnit, and Clifford Yorke—found important enough to participate in: (1) Heinz Hartmann, together with a small group of coworkers (René Spitz, Edith Jacobson, Margaret Mahler, Anna Freud, Ernst Kris, and Kurt Eissler), all of them refugees from Europe, so transformed American psychoanalysis as to justify the term “the Hartmann era.” (2) With the exception of the Hampstead Clinic, Anna Freud's center in London, the ideas of the Hartmann group did not find a foothold in other countries. (3) The Hartmann era, with its concepts and vocabulary, has faded from the American scene. (4) If the analysis of these events is correct, psychoanalytic history is more significantly influenced by cultural changes then we have hitherto been ready to face. There is a dire need for a history of psychoanalysis as a history of ideas, and this book was a step in that direction.

Brenner disagrees, considering my view “too parochial” and “focused exclusively on New York.” We both will have to await the verdict of history. As if in support of my position, the very same issue of JAPA contains a paper by Robert Wallerstein, “The Growth and Transformation of American Ego Psychology,” which in all essentials agrees with my point of view. I've written this reply because I believe that readers of JAPA should be made aware of these issues.

The mind's ingrained need to reduce and resolve conflicting perspectives, it is argued, is at the root of struggles among “schools” of analytic thought. Focusing attention on the conflictual process in analytic work can help both patients and analysts appreciate the fundamentally complementary nature of mind and the value of embracing distinctive points of view.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic Patients in the U.S., Canada, and Australia

II. A DSM-III-R Validation Study

Norman Doidge , Barry Simon , William Lancee , Michael First , Jacqueline Brunshaw , Lee Brauer , Donald Grant , Annette Stevens , John Oldham and Paul Mosher

Paul Mosher

180 Bloor Street West (#501)
Toronto, Ontario, M5S 2V6
Canada
Fax:416-783-8556
E-mail: Norman.doidge@utoronto.ca

Paul Mosher  

A study was conducted to validate our previous work on the DSM-III-R disorders diagnosed in patients in psychoanalysis in the U.S., Canada, and Australia and to determine which specific mood, anxiety, and personality disorders were the most common in these patients. The earlier study consisted of three surveys of psychoanalytic practice that together obtained data on 1,718 patients, through extensive mail surveys to analysts in the three countries. In the validation study, 206 patients were diagnosed using a different technique. Analysts similar in important respects to those who participated in the original surveys rated patients diagnostically before and after DSM-III-R training. After training, no significant changes appeared in the rates for any of the specific mood disorders. For the thirty disorders examined, training effects decreased the identification of the generalized anxiety disorder, and increased the identification of three personality disorders: avoidant, dependent, and personality disorder not otherwise specified. Thus, analysts slightly underdiagnosed the number of personality disorders, and some “anxious” patients appear to have qualified for personality disorders. Some limitations of the DSM-III-R notion of narcissistic personality are discussed, as are the importance and stability of the self-defeating (masochistic) personality disorder. The most common Axis I disorder in psychoanalytic patients was dysthymia, followed by major depression, recurrent. This study reinforces the findings of the original three surveys. Minor corrections were developed to adjust the original three surveys.

responseSet.title: Where's the Text?: The Problem of Validation in Psychoanalysis
responseSet.authorMast: Paul Siegel,  Lawrence Josephs and  Joel Weinberger
responseSet.authorCitation: Siegel, P., Josephs, L. & Weinberger, J.
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Where's the Text?

The Problem of Validation in Psychoanalysis

Paul Siegel , Lawrence Josephs and Joel Weinberger

Joel Weinberger

403 East 64th Street #5A
New York, NY 10021
E-mail: Psiegel68@yahoo.com

Joel Weinberger  

Whatever their differences, the various schools of psychoanalysis all subscribe to the case study, based on clinical vignette and anecdote, as the “text” of psychoanalysis. Because the typical case study presents not the actual analytic dialogue but a reconstruction based on the analyst's selective memory, the use of such a text renders problematic the validation of an analyst's case formulation, and has traditionally diminished the epistemological status of psychoanalysis. Pristine clinical data, as would be provided by verbatim transcripts of audio-recorded analytic sessions, are rarely created and even more rarely made public. Study of the psychoanalytic process is thereby made impossible, whether by clinical exegesis, qualitative analysis, or quantitative statistical analysis. Yet the analytic community, highly resistant to this public documentation, continues to marshal five common arguments against it. These arguments are reviewed and responded to in the belief that such documentation would enhance the field's intellectual status by allowing the problem of clinical validation to be addressed in ways that move beyond appeals to the authority of private clinical experience.

The Interpretive Attitude

Warren Poland

Warren Poland

5225 Connecticut Avenue
Washington, DC 20015
Fax: 301-229-0333
E-mail: wspmd@erols.com

Warren Poland  

From its start, psychoanalysis took as its primary goal the gaining of insight, and the analyst's interpretations as its primary technique for achieving that goal. Multiple factors, including the destructive impact of wild analysis and a growing appreciation of noninterpretive analytic functions, have brought those first principles into question. The author posits a conceptual division of the analyst's interpretive functions into declarative interpretations (content) and a procedural interpretive attitude (underlying process). The interpretive attitude is presented as crucial and central to psychoanalytic work, relevant and effective even when matters touching on preverbal issues are addressed.

Classics Revisited

Freud's the Ego and the Id and “Inhibitions, Symptoms and Anxiety”

Norman Doidge

Norman Doidge

180 Bloor Street West (# 501)
Toronto, Ontario, M5S 2V6
Canada
Fax: 416-783-8556

Norman Doidge  

Charles Hanly, who chaired the panel, began by pointing out that these two classics have been profitably revisited many times, and remain at the heart of contemporary psychoanalysis, though they were not always appreciated as they now are. A critic at the time The Ego and the Id was published said it was a decline in Freud's thinking and was lacking in ideas; it was, Hanly noted, described as “unclear, artificially assembled, and positively loathsome in its diction,” as well as being “displeasing apart from the fundamental idea of the id, and the aperçu of the genesis of morality.” That critic was the author himself, in a letter to Ferenczi. Freud was his own most demanding taskmaster. These works are examples of Freud's ability to test, question, and modify, in the light of new data, theories he had used for many years.

Freud's “aperçu of the genesis of morality” is a remarkable achievement best seen from the perspective of the history of ideas, according to Hanly, the fundamental movement of which has been from animism and magic, through religion and metaphysics, to science, naturalism, and technology. During the course of human psychic and cultural evolution, the ability to perform actions for the sake of moral duty developed. This deontological capacity, to do what is right because it is the right thing to do, mystified the human intellect for centuries. It was believed linked to a transcendental spiritual order and to vouchsafe to those who exercised it the reward of immortality. Plato traced its origins to a supernatural preexistence of the soul.

Panel held at the Spring Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, Toronto, May 30, 1998.

Panelists: Charles M.T. Hanly (chair), Charles Brenner, Ruth Imber, Arnold H. Modell, Theodore Shapiro.

December 15, 2001. Although “Psychoanalysis and Schizophrenia: A Cautionary Tale” (J. Amer. Psychoanal. Assn.49/1) was really not about the etiology of schizophrenia, the three responses printed above are concerned mainly with that issue. I was using the illness of schizophrenia as an example of where, in psychoanalytic theory building, the deficiencies in the mother-child dyad in the first few years of life have been considered crucial to the etiology of a disorder. I was asking the question, How do we know what we believe we know about etiology? Can we reliably use inferences drawn from psychoanalytic treatment to conclude that early difficulties are primarily responsible for an illness?

responseSet.title: Words and Wordlessness in the Psychoanalytic Situation
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Words and Wordlessness in the Psychoanalytic Situation

Donnel Stern

Donnel Stern

24 East 82nd Street
New York, NY 10028
E-mail: Sternhift@aol.com

Donnel Stern  

The difference between words and wordlessness in the psychoanalytic situation is examined in the context of a detailed clinical example. Various pairs of terms that have been used to account for this difference are mapped onto it: word and act, thought and feeling, public and private experience. Each of these sets of differences suggests certain relations between consciousness and the unconscious, and each implies a position about the nature of language. All three prove to be incomplete or inadequate ways of accounting for the difference between words and wordlessness in the clinical setting. The divergence, though, is well described by the difference between self-reflection and unformulated experience. Reflection is then presented as situated in the unformulated background meanings that contextualize it. It is because of this contextual embeddedness that self-reflection can have the kind of depth, resonance, power, and nuance more commonly associated with the nonverbal. In the creation of an explicit meaning, we simultaneously reconfigure the wordless background, thereby creating new possibilities for other meanings.

responseSet.title: Quantitative Research on the Primary Process: Method and Findings
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Quantitative Research on the Primary Process

Method and Findings

Robert Holt

Robert Holt

P.O. Box 1087
Truro, MA 02666
Fax: 508-349-2120
E-mail: Bobholt@capecod.net

Robert Holt  

Freud always defined the primary process metapsychologically, but he described the ways it shows up in dreams, parapraxes, jokes, and symptoms with enough observational detail to make it possible to create an objective, reliable scoring system to measure its manifestations in Rorschach responses, dreams, TAT stories, free associations, and other verbal texts. That system can identify signs of the thinker's efforts, adaptive or maladaptive, to control or defend against the emergence of primary process. A prerequisite and a consequence of the research that used this system was clarification and elaboration of the psychoanalytic theory of thinking. Results of empirical tests of several propositions derived from psychoanalytic theory are summarized. Predictions concerning the method's most useful index, of adaptive vs. maladaptive regression, have been repeatedly verified: People who score high on this index (who are able to produce well-controlled “primary products” in their Rorschach responses), as compared to those who score at the maladaptive pole (producing primary-process-filled responses with poor reality testing, anxiety, and pathological defensive efforts), are better able to tolerate sensory deprivation, are more able to enter special states of consciousness comfortably (drug-induced, hypnotic, etc.), and have higher achievements in artistic creativity, while schizophrenics tend to score at the extreme of maladaptive regression. Capacity for adaptive regression also predicts success in psychotherapy, and rises with the degree of improvement after both psychotherapy and drug treatment. Some predictive failures have been theoretically interesting: Kris's hypothesis about creativity and the controlled use of primary process holds for males but usually not for females. This body of work is presented as a refutation of charges, brought by such critics as Crews, that psychoanalysis cannot become a science.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Future of Psychoanalytic Research

Turning Points and New Opportunities

Stuart Hauser

Stuart Hauser

3 Blackfan Circle
Boston, MA 02115-5794
Fax: 617-730-5440
E-mail: Shauser@jbcc.harvard.edu

Stuart Hauser  

The fiftieth anniversary of the Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association presents a special occasion for an issue celebrating the growth and diversity of psychoanalytic research. We are currently witness to an array of rigorous inquiries targeting psychoanalytic theory, processes, practice, and outcomes (see, e.g., Bucci 2001; Fonagy 2000; Luborsky et al. 2001; Luborsky and Crits-Christoph 1990; Shapiro and Emde 1995). This rich landscape represents the fruition of much successful collaboration across several related disciplines—developmental psychology, cognitive psychology, clinical psychology, neuropsychology, and psycholinguistics. Psychoanalytic investigators are increasingly engaged in the interdisciplinary boundary crossing urged on us a decade ago by Holzman and Aronson (1992) in their challenging paper, “Psychoanalysis and Its Neighboring Sciences: Paradigms and Opportunities.”

More recently this motif surfaced again, in Bucci's reflections (2001) on the implications of several research reports. Emphasizing the need for psychoanalysis to further enhance its links with neighboring disciplines and to continue building bridges between psychoanalytic investigators and other scientists, she speaks of a “psychoanalytic science”:

Such a science needs to draw on related fields … and in return has much to offer them. There needs to be renewed emphasis on a theoretical framework… within which the concepts of psychoanalysis may be systematically defined, in terms of one another and in relation to observable events, and within which measures, purportedly of related or similar constructs, may be compared. Normative data are needed on relevant variables to allow meaningful and reliable interpretation of results in clinical groups. Also needed is an increased emphasis on ethnic and cultural diversity … [p. 66].

395

[[RunningHead]]

The Termination Phase of Psychoanalysis as Seen Through the Lens of the Dream

Gary Grenell

Gary Grenell

3121 East Madison Street
Suite 208B
Seattle, WA 98112-4260
Fax: 206-328-0284
E-mail: Mail@garygrenell.com

Gary Grenell  

The decision about when to terminate analysis has long been underpinned by a theory-driven criterion model, which may steer the analytic dialogue away from its customary activities of free association, empathic listening, and interpretation. As a remedy to this situation, the author proposes that by paying careful attention to less consciously crafted patient communications such as dreams, the analytic dyad can consider readiness to set a termination date from a perspective that is context-sensitive and less encumbered by preordained criteria. Tracing the dreams of one analysand from the vantage point of contemporary dream theory, the author demonstrates how careful attention to the dream elucidated the patient's readiness to terminate and her complex feelings about the termination process. Finally, the author challenges the notion that the termination phase is of greater evaluative than therapeutic importance, and provides clinical material as evidence that this is not the case.

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Growth and Transformation of American Ego Psychology

Robert Wallerstein

Robert Wallerstein

290 Beach Road
Belvedere, CA 94920
Fax: 415-789-9530

Robert Wallerstein  

The roots of ego psychology trace back to Sigmund Freud's The Ego and the Id (1923) and “Inhibitions, Symptoms and Anxiety” (1926), works followed by two additional fundaments, Anna Freud's The Ego and the Mechanisms of Defense (1936) and Heinz Hartmann's Ego Psychology and the Problem of Adaptation (1939). It was brought to full flowering in post-World War II America by Hartmann and his many collaborators, and for over two decades it maintained a monolithic hegemony over American psychoanalysis. Within this framework the conceptions of the psychoanalytic psychotherapies evolved as specific modifications of psychoanalytic technique directed to the clinical needs of the spectrum of patients not amenable to psychoanalysis proper. This American consensus on the ego psychology paradigm and its array of technical implementations fragmented several decades ago, with the rise in America of Kohut's self psychology, geared to the narcissistic disorders, and with the importation from Britain of neo-Kleinian and object-relational perspectives, all coinciding with the rapid growth of the varieties of relational psychoanalysis, with its shift in focus to the two-person, interactive, and co-constructed transference-countertransference matrix. Implications of this intermingled theoretical pluralism (as contrasted with the unity of the once dominant ego psychology paradigm) for the evolution of the American ego psychology are spelled out.

Heinz Kohut: The Making of a Psychoanalyst.

Charles B. Strozier. New York: Farrar Straus & Giroux, 2001, 495 pp., $35.00

Geoffrey Cocks

Geoffrey Cocks

Albion, Ml 49224
Fax: 517-629-0428
E-mail: Gcocks@albion.edu

Geoffrey Cocks  

According to Charles Strozier, “There was no one like [Heinz] Kohut who so intensely idealized psychoanalysis itself, who lived off it, who was so central to its institutional structures, and who then challenged it at such a fundamental level” (p. 143). Strozier, a professor of history at John Jay College and a training and supervising analyst at the Training and Research Institute for Self Psychology in New York, argues in this rich and authoritative biography of Kohut that “with his own protean sexuality and identity confusions, he fit uneasily into the world of psychoanalysis as Freud constructed it …, [though] for all his own confusions and contradictions, [he] may well have saved psychoanalysis from itself” (p. x). This is, appropriately, a very psychoanalytic thesis, one that Strozier, with a professional background in history and self psychology, as well as personal acquaintance with Kohut and many of his key followers, makes a strong case for.

responseSet.title: Psychic Change in Analysis: Its Relation to Analyst's and Patient's Goals
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychic Change in Analysis

Its Relation to Analyst's and Patient's Goals

David Raphling

David Raphling

David Raphling  

Patient (after two years of analysis): I think I'm done.

Analyst: You're only half done. We need to turn you over and do the other side.

In the minds of both analyst and patient, analytic progress carries with it a notion of an ultimate outcome. As the highly personal investments of analyst and patient in their preferred analytic goals solidify, the similarities and differences between their goals become more apparent. Their individual aims for analytic cure can be a source of strain, so that patients may become reluctant to assume responsibility for their own goals and attribute them instead to the analyst. The actual expectations and influence of analysts, as well as the expectations and influence attributed to them by patients, have the beneficial effect of reinforcing psychic change. A patient's identification with the analyst's goals may be a defense against the dangers of self-determination. Although this must ultimately be analyzed, it may be temporarily accepted by both parties as part of an adaptive, incremental process of psychic transformation.

From the very beginning of an analysis, through the decision to terminate, and to the end of the last hour, an analyst offers a patient both understanding and direction through consistent lines of interpretation of pathogenic conflicts. Whatever their content, analytic interventions convey expectations of the patient, which eventually include the goals for the analysis that the analyst deems appropriate.

David L. Raphling was a Training and Supervising Analyst at the Washington Psychoanalytic Institute. At the time of his death on March 28, 2001, he was Chairman of the Education Committee of the Washington Institute, and Clinical Professor of Psychiatry at the United States Uniformed Health Services School of Medicine.

Submitted for Publication March 9, 2000.

765

[[RunningHead]]

Being Gay and Becoming a Psychoanalyst

Three Generations

Michael Feldman

Michael Feldman

350 Central Park West
Suite 2H
New York, NY 10025
E-mail: Feldman@childpsych.columbia.edu

Michael Feldman  

Ross introduced the panel by locating the topic in its historical context: the second half of the Freudian Century. For much of this period, faculty at the Columbia Psychoanalytic Center, including Rado, Ovesey, and Socarides, pathologized homosexuality. Eventually more contemporary thinkers such as Isay, Downey, and Friedman began to normalize it, and Columbia, still a leader, was the first institute within the American Psychoanalytic Association to graduate an openly gay woman and man. Ross himself now believes that analysts blinded themselves with their own heterocentrist thinking. “When it came to homosexual individuals, theoretically and clinically,” Ross said, “round pegs were jammed into square holes, often splintering, with disastrous consequences.” In contrast, he pointed to extra-analytic research on the unfolding of sexual orientation (most recently surveyed in Cohler and Galatzer-Levy 2000) that reports neither universal causes nor universal correlates “in the course of gay and lesbian lives.”

The panel that followed consisted of autobiographical presentations from gay analysts of three generations. The distinctly different time periods when these men trained correspond to different periods in the history of the relationship between psychoanalysis, with its homophobic attitudes, and lesbian and gay individuals. Two discussions followed the panel, one by a lesbian psychoanalyst and one by a gay psychoanalytic historian. The reporter offered a brief commentary on the current status of gays and lesbians at Columbia, and this was followed by a discussion among the panelists and members of the audience.

Panel held at the Spring Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, New Orleans, May 2001.

Panelists: John Munder Ross (chair); Ralph Roughton, Jack Drescher, and Ubaldo Leli (presenters); Susan C. Vaughan and Ken Lewes (discussants).

Psychoanalysis and Schizophrenia

Three Responses to Martin Willick

William Gottdiener

William Gottdiener

First Avenue at 16th Street
New York, NY 10003
E-mail: Wgottdiener@chpnet.org

William Gottdiener  

April 12, 2001. Is the cause of schizophrenia primarily biological, with little or no contribution from psychosocial factors, as Martin Willick (J. Amer. Psychoanal. Assn.49/1) suggests? Although there is clearly a biological contribution to the etiology of schizophrenia, there is growing evidence that psychosocial factors—particularly childhood physical and sexual abuse—play an extremely important role in its etiology (for a review of the literature, see Read 1997). Traumatic experiences like these might not be a contributing factor in all people diagnosed with schizophrenia, but approximately 60 percent of schizophrenic patients report having experienced some form of childhood abuse (Read 1997). Further, neurological data strongly suggest that traumatic experiences like these have an untoward impact on the developing brain, which, it has been hypothesized, might create the vulnerability to developing schizophrenia (Read 2001).

It should be clear by now that child abuse does not necessarily implicate families as its perpetrators. Families are not the only possible source of such abuse: childhood traumas can occur under a variety of circumstances with many different people.

[[RunningHead]]

The Emergent Ego: Complexity and Coevolution in the Psychoanalytic Process

Stanley R. Palombo. Madison, CT: International Universities Press, 1999, 395 pp., $65.00.

Emmanuel Ghent

Emmanuel Ghent

131 Prince Street
New York, NY 10012
Fax 212-477-2644
E-mail: Ghent@psychoanalysis.net

Emmanuel Ghent  

The book begins just so: “There is something new under the sun in psychoanalysis.” With this exciting opener, Stanley Palombo heralds a new conceptual universe for psychoanalysis. This wonderful and timely book is both very complex and very simple. In its overall thrust the message is straightforward: Palombo recasts the psychoanalytic process as an evolutionary process. Much of the book is occupied with spelling out in compelling detail what this means. He is not using the term evolutionary process lightly. He first invites the reader to join him in a sophisticated exploration of contemporary evolutionary theory, focusing particularly on the self-emergent properties of the nonlinear cause-and-effect relations that characterize complexity theory. From here, Palombo takes the reader on a remarkable journey whose overall purpose is to reveal how the new science of complexity offers to psychoanalysis a way of thinking about the psychoanalytic process as coevolutionary, a process in which analyst and patient evolve together much as an ecological system does. The psychoanalytic partners form a loosely coupled self-organizing system, the components of which are themselves tightly coupled self-organizing systems. Palombo advises the reader not to equate the implied gradualism of Darwinian evolution of species with the matter at hand.

352

Jorge Ahumada

Jorge Ahumada  

February 26, 2001. Mitchell Wilson bases his letter on the notion that the writer of an essay provides the uninitiated reader an explanation of basic tenets (in the present case, of Lacanism), starting from assumedly “clear principles and assumptions,” leaving cultural and political contexts aside. Thus, he demands a sweet and easy explication of Lacanism—presumably one built on its own tenets. Were such admirable didactic simplicity attainable, one would be hard put to understand that Lacan's disciple Jacques Perrier (1985) can say in his Voyages Extraordinaires en Translacanie that Lacan never managed to pinpoint such keystone principles as the “signifier,” or the fact that Lacan himself complained, in one of his last public appearences, that he felt persecuted by misunderstandings, and that the more he tried to undo them the more he exacerbated them (Julien 1986, p. 11). For this reason, it seemed to me prudent to allow Leclaire the space to display his own explanations.

To his credit, his attempt at clinically demonstrating the Lacanian conception of the “signifier” is the clearest and most comprehensive such exposition I know of, this being why I presented it at some length. Nonetheless, it does little to help the Lacanian concept of “signifier” hold any water.

Lacan did start writing in the thirties, as Wilson notes, but he should be aware also that Lacan initially became a “celebrity” in Paris literary circles based on his early blending of surrealism and phenomenological psychiatry; he was known there as “the new Mallarmé”—a title not gratuitously bestowed. Roudinesco (1993), a disciple and a close friend of the Lacan family, can be profitably consulted on these matters.

To uncover the conceptual structures driven by postmodern rhetorics is not quite on a level with the typical book essay. It involves delving into universes quite alien from our usual, shared, evidence-driven psychoanalytic concerns. In this postmodern ambiance the psychoanalytic experience takes second place to “discourse” as a sacred practice of transmission (Levallois 1981), advanced and celebrated as a revolutionary language (Bourreaux-Hartemann 1981).

Anne Erreich

Anne Erreich

22 Riverside Drive
New York, NY 10023
E-mail: Aerreich@compuserve.com

Anne Erreich   faculty, NYU Psychoanalytic Institute; Clinical Associate Professor, Department of Psychiatry, NYU Medical Center.

The plural of anecdote is data.

—Biologist Marc Bekoff on The Debate Regarding The Existence of Complex Emotions in Animals

We psychoanalysts occupy a position in scientific circles a bit like that of the 61 million pet owners in the United States. Armed with a wealth of anecdotal data, they are convinced that Fido and Fluffy can feel anger, jealousy, depression, joy, even embarrassment, but their anecdotal reports have not convinced scientific researchers.

In the past hundred years, psychoanalysts too have accumulated a wealth of case studies from which we have extrapolated a somewhat rickety, unwieldy, sprawling, and often internally inconsistent theory of mind, development, and clinical practice. The resulting discipline and its adherents' behavior have generated a history almost Fellini-esque in its passionate, idiosyncratic, brawling, and schismatic nature, the very reflection of the unruly human passions that are its object of study. At this point in our history, notions thought to be the bedrock of the discipline-drives, free association, the anonymity and neutrality of the analyst-are being challenged. Well, it's been a fun hundred years, but where do we go from here?

One answer is offered by the two volumes under review, both part of a series devoted to empirical studies in psychoanalysis. Editors Robert Bornstein and Joseph Masling propose to bridge the clinician-researcher gap by integrating clinical anecdotes and research data to provide enriched psychoanalytic models of mind and clinical practice.

657

Howard Levine   faculty, Psychoanalytic Institute of New England, East and Massachusetts Institute for Psychoanalysis.

Belief and Imagination is written within a contemporary Kleinian framework, but it should be accessible to analysts of any persuasion. The starting point for these explorations is a set of questions that unify and inform a wide range of subjects of analytic interest: 1) What is “the status of phantasies in the mind of the individual? … When are they regarded as facts, probabilities, possibilities or mere fancies?” (p. 1); What is the “internal relationship of subjectivity to objectivity” (p. 1) and how does it originate and develop in the primitive oedipal triangle; and 3) What and where is the Imagination, and how is it to be construed in any modern model of the mind?

Intrinsic to these questions is the individual's relationship to knowledge, the search for which Britton accords full instinctual status: “I regard the epistemophilic instinct (Wissentrieb) to be on a par with and independent of the other instincts.… The desire for knowledge exists alongside love and hate. Human beings have an urge to love, to hate, to know, and a desire to be loved, a fear of being hated, and a wish to be understood.… Belief, as an act, is in the realm of knowledge what attachment is in the realm of love” (pp. 11-12).

Knowledge, however, can be oriented toward and used in the service of either phantasy or reality. Britton emphasizes the importance of our individual capacities to accept and acknowledge whatever degree of uncertainty may inhere in what we “know.” Thus, he proffers belief as a more humble form of knowing than fact, and he suggests that our awareness that what we possess is a belief rather than a fact is a function of how well we can accept limits on our omniscient grandiosity: “Claiming to know

On Bearing Unbearable States of Mind

Ruth Riesenberg-Malcolm, edited by Priscilla Roth. London: Routledge, 1999, 201 pp., $95 hardcover, $32.95 paperback

Abbot Bronstein

Abbot Bronstein

2473 Washington Street
San Francisco, CA 94115
E-mail: Cladg@aol.com

Abbot Bronstein   San Francisco Psychoanalytic Institute.

On Bearing Unbearable States of Mind is the thirty-fourth book in the New Library of Psychoanalysis series. Many books in this series offer compilations of papers spanning many years of their authors' work. In the case of Ruth Riesenberg-Malcolm's book, the reader is treated to twenty-five years of her writings, in which one can see the development and consistency of her theoretical and clinical ideas.

A collection of papers does not always provide the smooth and coherent evolution of topics that a monograph does, and that is true here. However, this is a minor criticism of an otherwise useful collection of papers, excellently organized and edited by Priscilla Roth. Readers of this book will be aided by Roth's succinct reviews of the papers and the points of focus that she highlights, particularly in parts I and II. Some of Riesenberg-Malcolm's papers have been updated for this publication, and they are gathered here according to subject matter rather then chronologically. Although this is a useful organization for the book, the sections are not neatly separated and the distinctions between them are at times difficult to ascertain.

While Riesenberg-Malcolm is not as well known to many in the United States as some of the other contemporary Kleinian writers, her writings are read widely in England, Europe, and South America. She herself is a native of South America (Chile), and trained in the British Psychoanalytic Institute within the Kleinian group. In her various papers we can see close ties to Bion, Segal, and Joseph in particular, and also the extension of some of the ideas of Steiner and Britton.

In my attempts to review a book with the scope of Riesenberg-Malcolm's, I was most struck by the details of the her clinical work with the array of patients that constitutes an analyst's practice.

1052

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Kleinian Theory: A Contemporary Perspective

edited by Catalina Bronstein. London: WHURR, 2001, xxi + 211 pp.; $38.95 paperback, American edition.

Peter Lawner

Peter Lawner

187 Mt. Auburn Street, # 2
Cambridge, MA 02138
E-mail: Ptrlwnr@aol.com

Peter Lawner   Faculty and Supervising Analyst, Center for Psychoanalytic Studies, Massachusetts General Hospital; Faculty, Massachusetts Institute for Psychoanalysis; Assistant Clinical Professor, Department of Psychiatry, Harvard Medical School.

In recent years, American analysts have been dramatically extending the boundaries around what they consider acceptable analytic theory. In some psychoanalytic circles, however, although not in all, they have remained notably reserved about including Kleinian theory and clinical method. There was an illustration of this skeptical view in the late 1990s, when a well-known British Kleinian gave a clinical

1061

presentation at an eminent institute of the American, to the general disapproval of discussant and audience. What accounts for the continued hesitation of an important segment of the ever more pluralistic American psychoanalytic community to credit Kleinian conceptualization and praxis?

Beyond the fact that Kleinian conceptions and clinical methods markedly diverge from ego analytic ones (see Zetzel 1956; she was one of the few American analysts to have had extensive firsthand experience in England with Kleinian thought and practice), at least two other influences continue to dispose some American analysts to experience Kleinians and their ideas as “other.” The political struggles between Anna Freud and Melanie Klein that culminated in the Controversial Discussions in London in the early forties cemented their opposition to Kleinian innovations and foreclosed their potential responsiveness to them. Moreover, the geographical divide separating the United States and London, the heartland of creative Kleinian activity, means that the three pillars of analytic apprenticeship—personal (training) analysis, supervised (control) analyses, and didactic learning (coursework)—are only very limitedly available, in their Kleinian incarnations, in the United States. Authentic “feel” for the Kleinian perspective requires immersion in and experience of its clinical efficacy, which cannot be achieved via the occasional Kleinian lecture and workshop. The literature consequently remains the avenue most continuously available to the American analyst who wants to absorb Kleinian understandings. The volume I consider here is a well realized and valuable contribution for those motivated to enrich their appreciation of the Kleinian perspective.

PEP Archive 1 CD-ROM, Version 3, 1920-1998: Full Article Text

Psychoanalytic Electronic Publishing, Inc.2001. New customer $1470; Updates from $300.00-600.00

Sydney Pulver

Sydney Pulver

111 N. 49th Street
Philadelphia, PA 19139
Fax: 215-472-7716
E-mail: Spulver@mail.med.upenn.edu

Sydney Pulver   Training and Supervising Analyst, Psychoanalytic Center of Philadelphia; Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, University of Pennsylvania School of Medicine.

For anyone with a serious interest in psychoanalysis, this CD-ROM is indispensable. The PEP (Psychoanalytic Electronic Publishing) Archive, version 3, contains all of the articles published between 1920 and 1998 in the following journals: Contemp. Psychoanal. (vols. 1-34: 1964-1998); Int. Rev. Psycho-Anal. (vols. 1-19: 1974-1992); Int. J. Psycho-Anal. (vols. 1-79: 1920-1998); J. Amer. Psychoanal. Assn. (vols. 1-46: 1953-1998); Psychoanal. Dial. (vols. 1-8: 1991-1998); Psychoanal. Inq. (vols. 1-18: 1981-1998); Psychoanal. Q. (vols. 1-67: 1932-1998); and Psychoanal. St. Child (vols. 1-53: 1945-1998).

The disc makes available nearly the entirety of the psychoanalytic periodical literature; even books are accessible indirectly through the book reviews contained on the PEP CD. The Standard Edition is not yet included, but Freud's work after 1920 appears in English as it was originally published in the International Journal and the Quarterly. All of this rich content can be accessed through a sophisticated search engine, which enables one to search everything on the CD-ROM in a variety of ways. The state-of-the-art disc truly revolutionizes the approach to the psychoanalytic literature. Gone are the hours of library searches, photocopying, and attempting to obtain articles that no one seems to have. Gone is the frustration of finding out after all this work that someone else has already written on your topic, only better. (Of course, we may still find that out—or that something has not been written about enough—but with less waste of time.)

As well as for facilitating research, the disc is invaluable for supervision.

1091

responseSet.title: The Theory of Psychoanalysis: Vicissitudes of its Evolution
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Theory of Psychoanalysis

Vicissitudes of its Evolution

Leo Rangell

Leo Rangell

456 North Carmelina Avenue
Los Angeles, CA 90049
Fax: 310-472-6616
E-mail: Leormd@ucla.edu

Leo Rangell  

The apex of psychoanalysis is its theory. Everything else stems from the understanding this provides, from the technical procedure that derives from it to the huge literature of applied analysis. Since my invitation to write this paper on the state of psychoanalysis was extended on the basis of my seniority and survival, as one of the few living contributors to the first volume of this journal, and because I have been personally involved in the history of this theory for over half of “the Century of Freud,” my aim is to present an overview of its development, an aerial view as it were, of the forest rather than the trees. While details will thus not be the subject, what will be gained is an orientation toward large movements, whose relations to each other are usually obscured from view.

In my title I say “the” theory of psychoanalysis quite deliberately, which brings us at once to the substance of this overview. Against the pluralism widely held today, I believe that the century gave birth to a single unified theory of psychoanalysis, not several or many. That theory stands in contrast to more limited explanations of mental life—organic, cognitive, behavioral, existential—each focusing on one or another aspect of mentation and behavior. Psychoanalysis includes all of these, but as it developed into a superordinate theory, it added a specific, idiosyncratic sine qua non of its own. Looking for its essence, the core that distinguishes psychoanalysis from other social or psychological sciences, I found it in its exclusive domain of “unconscious

Training and Supervising Analyst, Los Angeles Psychoanalytic Institute; Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, UCLA; Clinical Professor of Psychiatry (Psychoanalysis), UC-San Francisco.

A briefer version of this paper, with an adaptation to child analysis, was presented as the John A. Hadden Jr., M.D., Memorial Award Lecture, the Hanna Perkins Center for Child Development, Cleveland, OH, April 26, 2002. Submitted for publication August 9, 2002.

Painting, Psychoanalysis and Spirituality

Stephen James Newton. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 2001, xvi + 264 pp., $75.00

Gilbert Rose

Gilbert Rose

Box 215
25 Sammis Street
Rowayton, CT 06853
Fax: 203-838-1906

Gilbert Rose  

This book is intended to correct what the author believes to be a glaring omission on the part of classical psychoanalysis: a consideration of the problem of aesthetic form, as opposed to content. Accordingly, Stephen Newton advances a theory of artistic creativity centering on the spiritual dimension of painting and based on Anton Ehrenzweig's adaptation of Kleinian psychology. Along the way, he provides illuminating discussions of contemporary artists such as Roger Hilton, Willem de Kooning, Philip Guston, and Georg Baselitz, and of icon painters, Fra Angelico, Leonardo, Nicholas Poussin, and Cézanne, among others.

Writing in the 1960s, Ehrenzweig proposed that the unconscious primary process is not chaotic but, rather, a layered structure. The deepest level, the “manic-oceanic,” is negotiable only through unconscious form. This is achieved when the creative process is able to incorporate unconscious psychic structure into the formal structure of painting. This deep, unconscious, manic-oceanic level of creative activity is the basis for the spiritual dimension of art—regarding which, Newton claims, both psychoanalysis and postmodern art are “in denial.

Storms in her Head: Freud and the Construction of Hysteria.

Muriel Dimen and Adrienne Harris. New York: Other Press, 2001, 401 pp., $25.00

Dianne Elise

Dianne Elise

5435 College Avenue, #106
Oakland, CA 94618

Dianne Elise  

As it was at the previous turn of the century, hysteria is once again a hot topic. Celebrating the centennial of Studies on Hysteria (Breuer and Freud 1893-1895), Muriel Dimen and Adrienne Harris have compiled a collection of sixteen papers, with a preface and introduction that are themselves substantive contributions. The editors, in introducing the volume, skillfully interweave the richly diverse themes elaborated by the contributors regarding the nature of the therapeutic dialogue, authority in doctor-patient roles, and the power of transference and counter-transference. Authors were given the task of reacting to any of the six women patients represented in the Studies, and the result is an interdisciplinary project including not only psychoanalysts of various orientations, but historians, philosophers, and literary critics. The essays are well-written, wide-ranging, and intellectually challenging.

They offer a strong historical perspective, and present “a complex Freud, neither hero nor villain” (Michael Roth, p. 170). With regard to the impact of the female protagonists, Dimen and Harris express the theme that echoes throughout the volume: “the patients, in struggling with their doctors, force their doctors to change, and, in changing, they
