[[RunningHead]]

Freud in the Pampas: The Emergence and Development of a Psychoanalytic Culture in Argentina.

Mariano Ben Plotkin. Stanford: Stanford University Press, 2001, 314 pp., $24.95

Nancy Hollander

Nancy Hollander

3338 Cabrillo Blvd.
Los Angeles, CA 90066
Fax: 310-636-1997
E-mail: Nchollander@attbi.com

Nancy Hollander  

In this comprehensive social and cultural history, Mariano Ben Plotkin offers a thoroughgoing exploration of what made Buenos Aires a mecca for psychoanalysis. Noting that Argentina has one of the highest concentrations of psychoanalysts in the world, he writes in his introduction, “From politicians to bank clerks, … soap opera stars to cab drivers, and even a few generals—everybody seems to use psychoanalytic language to express the concerns of everyday life” (p. 1).

The author, who directs the New York University site in Buenos Aires, grew up there with the idea that monthly expenses included rent, food, clothing, and a four-times-a- week psychoanalysis! It was only later, while a graduate student in the United States, that he realized how unique to his native Argentina such an assumption was. Intrigued, he set about to do research and write a book whose aim was to explain “the diffusion of psychoanalysis in a specific society as a general cultural phenomenon and to analyze the conditions for its dissemination” (p. 4).

A detailed and thoughtful writer, Plotkin at times tells his story chronologically; at others, thematically. He traces the history of Argentine psychoanalysis from its early appearance in the 1910s through the post-military dictatorship period of the mid 1980s, all the while attending to such specific topics as the emergence of psychology as a discipline and its close relationship to psychoanalysis. He explores the complex convergence of psychoanalysis and Marxist thought and praxis within the context of the highly politicized environment of Argentina. He discusses the Lacanian challenge to Argentine Kleinianism by situating it in the political crisis that ended in the seven-year Dirty War and produced the most repressive military dictatorship in its history.

1413

Death Of A “Jewish Science”: Psychoanalysis in the Third Reich

James E. Goggin and Eileen Brockman Goggin. West Lafayette: Purdue University Press, 2001, 242 pp., $35.95

Ruth Lax

Ruth Lax

1185 Park Avenue
New York, NY 10128
Fax: 212-348-2937
E-mail: Rlaxphd@aol.com

Ruth Lax  

Death of a “Jewish Science” provides a powerful warning of what can happen when self-interest as rationalized opportunism becomes the modus operandi. James and Eileen Goggin dedicate the book to the “values of love, truth, work and democracy.” This spirit permeates their approach. Their presentation of the history of psychoanalysis before Hitler's rise to power and during the Third Reich is meant to clarify the ways in which it was extinguished. They demonstrate that the ideas and worldview of the psychoanalytic movement did not survive in Nazi Germany.

In 1920 Karl Abraham and Max Eitingon established in Berlin the first psychoanalytic institute (BPI), which soon became the leading training center in Europe. It had the first public psychoanalytic polyclinic and teaching facility anywhere.

[[RunningHead]]

The Mark of Cain: Psychoanalytic Insight and the Psychopath.

Edited by J. Reid Meloy. Hillsdale, NJ: The Analytic Press, 2001, 368 pp., $55.00

Warren Poland

Warren Poland

Warren Poland  

Psychopathy, sociopathy, antisocial behavior, sadism, etc.—efforts to categorize manifestations of evil have never been simple. This volume brings together a series of relevant studies, from those of Aichhorn and Reich in the early thirties to the more recent work of Symington and Kernberg in the eighties. The long list of significant contributions collected here includes articles by Bowlby, Greenacre, Adelaide Johnson, Helene Deutsch, Winnicott, Betty Joseph, and the editor himself.

What makes this convenient anthology especially valuable is the way Meloy both ties together and adds to prior thinking. Beyond his paper “The Psychology of Wickedness,” he supplies particularly helpful introductions to the two sections of the book, “Development and Psychodynamics” and “Treatment, Risk Management, and Psychodiagnosis.” Much more than prefaces, these provide their own thoughtful integration of both theory and practice.

Despite Meloy's attention to psychopathic grandiosity, his views resemble Kernberg's more than Kohut's. For instance, he writes, “It is difficult to advance a theoretical understanding of psychopathy if pathological narcissism is considered only a developmental fixation” (p. 11). Controversy is inevitable whenever particular positions are taken, yet Meloy has brought together and personally enriched a volume of great value in the continuing psychoanalytic study of evil.

1429
